AI.motivation: Golay complementary pairs (GCPs) have been widely studied since 1951 and applied in communication systems, but there are limitations in constructing GCPs with flexible length ranges, especially for higher-order q-ary sequences.
AI.method: The authors use extended Boolean functions (EBFs) to directly construct GCPs, proving that existence of quaternary (q=4) GCPs of length M is equivalent to explicit constructibility of (4h)-ary GCPs of length 2^mM for all integers h,m≥1.
AI.result: The paper establishes an equivalence relationship between quaternary GCP existence and higher-order GCP constructibility, and provides direct construction methods yielding GCPs with more flexible length ranges than previous results.
AI.conclusion: The equivalence theorem provides a systematic approach to constructing higher-order GCPs from quaternary ones, and the EBF-based direct construction offers improved flexibility in length ranges for practical applications in communication systems.

title: Interpretable Human Activity Recognition for Subtle Robbery Detection in Surveillance Videos
summary: Non-violent street robberies (snatch-and-run) are difficult to detect automatically because they are brief, subtle, and often indistinguishable from benign human interactions in unconstrained surveillance footage. This paper presents a hybrid, pose-driven approach for detecting snatch-and-run events that combines real-time perception with an interpretable classification stage suitable for edge deployment. The system uses a YOLO-based pose estimator to extract body keypoints for each tracked person and computes kinematic and interaction features describing hand speed, arm extension, proximity, and relative motion between an aggressor-victim pair. A Random Forest classifier is trained on these descriptors, and a temporal hysteresis filter is applied to stabilize frame-level predictions and reduce spurious alarms. We evaluate the method on a staged dataset and on a disjoint test set collected from internet videos, demonstrating promising generalization across different scenes and camera viewpoints. Finally, we implement the complete pipeline on an NVIDIA Jetson Nano and report real-time performance, supporting the feasibility of proactive, on-device robbery detection.

AI.tldr: A pose-based system for detecting snatch-and-run robberies in surveillance footage using kinematic features and Random Forest classification, achieving real-time performance on edge devices.
AI.motivation: Non-violent street robberies (snatch-and-run) are difficult to detect automatically because they are brief, subtle, and often indistinguishable from benign human interactions in unconstrained surveillance footage.
AI.method: Hybrid pose-driven approach using YOLO-based pose estimator to extract body keypoints, computing kinematic and interaction features (hand speed, arm extension, proximity, relative motion), training Random Forest classifier, and applying temporal hysteresis filter for stabilization.
AI.result: Promising generalization across different scenes and camera viewpoints on staged dataset and internet videos, with real-time performance achieved on NVIDIA Jetson Nano.
AI.conclusion: The system supports feasibility of proactive, on-device robbery detection through interpretable classification suitable for edge deployment.

title: Batch Effects In Brain Foundation Model Embeddings
summary: Foundation models show strong potential for large-scale, high-dimensional biomedical applications, yet their ability to capture relevant neurobiological characteristics remains underexplored. We systematically evaluate embeddings from two neuroimaging foundation models, BrainLM and SwiFT, across multi-site fMRI datasets using a comprehensive evaluation framework. Our results show that foundation model embeddings encode substantial batch-related variability, often dominating diagnosis-related information across heterogeneous datasets. We further investigate how harmonization, applied to reduce batch effects, influences these embeddings. In addition, we find that BrainLM prefers to capture fine-grained regional activity, whereas SwiFT tends to represent interactions between regions, consistent with their respective model architectures. Our study highlights the importance of accounting for batch effects in foundation models and motivates future work on disentangling biologically meaningful signals from acquisition-related variability.

AI.tldr: Foundation model embeddings for neuroimaging capture substantial batch effects that can dominate diagnosis-related information, with different models capturing different neurobiological characteristics.
AI.motivation: While foundation models show promise for biomedical applications, their ability to capture relevant neurobiological characteristics in neuroimaging data remains underexplored, particularly regarding batch effects and diagnostic information.
AI.method: Systematically evaluated embeddings from two neuroimaging foundation models (BrainLM and SwiFT) across multi-site fMRI datasets using a comprehensive evaluation framework, and investigated how harmonization affects these embeddings.
AI.result: Foundation model embeddings encode substantial batch-related variability that often dominates diagnosis-related information; BrainLM captures fine-grained regional activity while SwiFT represents interactions between regions; harmonization influences these embeddings.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights the critical importance of accounting for batch effects in foundation models and motivates future work on disentangling biologically meaningful signals from acquisition-related variability.

title: CooperDrive: Enhancing Driving Decisions Through Cooperative Perception
summary: Autonomous vehicles equipped with robust onboard perception, localization, and planning still face limitations in occlusion and non-line-of-sight (NLOS) scenarios, where delayed reactions can increase collision risk. We propose CooperDrive, a cooperative perception framework that augments situational awareness and enables earlier, safer driving decisions. CooperDrive offers two key advantages: (i) each vehicle retains its native perception, localization, and planning stack, and (ii) a lightweight object-level sharing and fusion strategy bridges perception and planning. Specifically, CooperDrive reuses detector Bird's-Eye View (BEV) features to estimate accurate vehicle poses without additional heavy encoders, thereby reconstructing BEV representations and feeding the planner with low latency. On the planning side, CooperDrive leverages the expanded object set to anticipate potential conflicts earlier and adjust speed and trajectory proactively, thereby transforming reactive behaviors into predictive and safer driving decisions. Real-world closed-loop tests at occlusion-heavy NLOS intersections demonstrate that CooperDrive increases reaction lead time, minimum time-to-collision (TTC), and stopping margin, while requiring only 90 kbps bandwidth and maintaining an average end-to-end latency of 89 ms.

AI.tldr: CooperDrive is a cooperative perception framework that enhances autonomous vehicle safety in occlusion/NLOS scenarios through lightweight object-level sharing and fusion, enabling earlier collision avoidance with minimal bandwidth/latency.
AI.motivation: Autonomous vehicles still struggle with occlusion and non-line-of-sight scenarios where limited visibility causes delayed reactions and increased collision risk. Current systems need better situational awareness in challenging visual conditions.
AI.method: CooperDrive uses a cooperative perception framework that: 1) maintains each vehicle's native perception/localization/planning stack, 2) employs lightweight object-level sharing and fusion, 3) reuses detector BEV features to estimate vehicle poses without extra encoders, 4) reconstructs BEV representations for planners, and 5) uses expanded object sets for earlier conflict anticipation and proactive speed/trajectory adjustments.
AI.result: Real-world tests at occlusion-heavy NLOS intersections show increased reaction lead time, higher minimum time-to-collision (TTC), improved stopping margin, while requiring only 90 kbps bandwidth and maintaining 89 ms average end-to-end latency.
AI.conclusion: CooperDrive successfully transforms reactive driving behaviors into predictive, safer decisions through cooperative perception, demonstrating practical feasibility with low bandwidth/latency requirements for real-world deployment in challenging occlusion scenarios.

title: Shapley Value-Guided Adaptive Ensemble Learning for Explainable Financial Fraud Detection with U.S. Regulatory Compliance Validation
comment: 28 pages. Submitted to Engineering Applications of Artificial Intelligence (Elsevier). IEEE-CIS dataset (590,540 transactions). Includes SGAE algorithm, SHAP stability evaluation, and OCC/SR 11-7 regulatory compliance mapping
summary: Financial crime costs U.S. institutions over $32 billion each year. Although AI tools for fraud detection have become more advanced, their use in real-world systems still faces a major obstacle: many of these models operate as black boxes that cannot provide the transparent, auditable explanations required by regulations such as OCC Bulletin 2011-12 and Federal Reserve SR 11-7. This study makes three main contributions. First, it offers a thorough evaluation of explanation quality across faithfulness (sufficiency and comprehensiveness at k=5, 10, and 15) and stability (Kendall's W across 30 bootstrap samples). XGBoost paired with TreeExplainer achieves near-perfect stability (W=0.9912), while LSTM with DeepExplainer shows weak results (W=0.4962). Second, the paper introduces the SHAP-Guided Adaptive Ensemble (SGAE), which dynamically adjusts per-transaction ensemble weights based on SHAP attribution agreement, achieving the highest AUC-ROC among all tested models (0.8837 held-out; 0.9245 cross-validation). Third, a complete three-architecture evaluation of LSTM, Transformer, and GNN-GraphSAGE on the full 590,540-transaction IEEE-CIS dataset is provided, with GNN-GraphSAGE achieving AUC-ROC 0.9248 and F1=0.6013. All results are mapped directly to OCC, SR 11-7, and BSA-AML regulatory compliance requirements.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the regulatory compliance challenge in AI fraud detection by evaluating explanation quality, introducing a SHAP-guided ensemble method, and benchmarking three architectures on a large financial dataset, with results mapped to regulatory requirements.
AI.motivation: Financial institutions face over $32B annual losses from crime, but AI fraud detection tools are often black boxes that don't meet regulatory requirements for transparency and auditability under OCC Bulletin 2011-12 and Federal Reserve SR 11-7.
AI.method: 1) Comprehensive evaluation of explanation quality using faithfulness metrics (sufficiency/comprehensiveness at k=5,10,15) and stability (Kendall's W across 30 bootstrap samples). 2) Introduction of SHAP-Guided Adaptive Ensemble (SGAE) that dynamically adjusts per-transaction ensemble weights based on SHAP attribution agreement. 3) Full evaluation of three architectures (LSTM, Transformer, GNN-GraphSAGE) on the 590,540-transaction IEEE-CIS dataset.
AI.result: XGBoost+TreeExplainer achieved near-perfect stability (W=0.9912), while LSTM+DeepExplainer showed weak stability (W=0.4962). SGAE achieved highest AUC-ROC (0.8837 held-out; 0.9245 CV). GNN-GraphSAGE performed best among architectures with AUC-ROC 0.9248 and F1=0.6013. All results mapped to regulatory compliance requirements.
AI.conclusion: The study provides a framework for regulatory-compliant AI fraud detection, demonstrating that explanation quality varies significantly across methods, that adaptive ensembles can improve performance, and that graph-based approaches show strong potential for financial fraud detection while maintaining explainability.

title: Formalizing Kantian Ethics: Formula of the Universal Law Logic (FULL)
summary: The field of machine ethics aims to build Artificial Moral Agents (AMAs) to better understand morality and make AI agents safer. To do so, many approaches encode human moral intuition as a set of axioms on actions e.g., do not harm, you must help others. However, this introduces (at least) two limitations for future AMAs. First, it does not consider the agent's purposes in performing the action. Second, it assumes that we humans can enumerate our moral intuition. This paper explores formalizing a moral procedure that alleviates these two limitations. We specifically consider Kantian ethics and present a multi-sorted quantified modal logic we call the Formula of the Universal Law Logic (FULL). The FULL formalizes Kant's first formulation of the categorical imperative, the Formula of the Universal Law (FUL), and concepts such as causality and agency. We demonstrate on three cases from Kantian ethics that the FULL can reason to evaluate agents' actions for certain purposes without built-in moral intuition, given that it has sufficient (non-normative) background knowledge. Therefore, the FULL is a contribution towards more robust and autonomous AMAs, and a more formal understanding of Kantian ethics.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a formal logic system called FULL (Formula of the Universal Law Logic) based on Kantian ethics to address limitations in current approaches to building Artificial Moral Agents (AMAs).
AI.motivation: Current approaches to machine ethics encode human moral intuition as axioms (e.g., "do not harm"), but this has two key limitations: 1) it ignores the agent's purposes in performing actions, and 2) assumes humans can fully enumerate moral intuitions. The paper aims to develop a more robust approach for autonomous moral reasoning.
AI.method: The authors develop a multi-sorted quantified modal logic called FULL (Formula of the Universal Law Logic) that formalizes Kant's first formulation of the categorical imperative (Formula of the Universal Law). The logic incorporates concepts like causality and agency to enable moral reasoning without relying on pre-defined moral axioms.
AI.result: The FULL system is demonstrated on three cases from Kantian ethics, showing it can evaluate agents' actions for specific purposes without built-in moral intuition, provided it has sufficient non-normative background knowledge.
AI.conclusion: FULL represents a contribution toward more robust and autonomous Artificial Moral Agents and provides a more formal understanding of Kantian ethics, addressing limitations of current axiom-based approaches.

title: Exploiting Correlations in Federated Learning: Opportunities and Practical Limitations
comment: 14 pages, 7 figures, submitted for possible publication
summary: The communication bottleneck in federated learning (FL) has spurred extensive research into techniques to reduce the volume of data exchanged between client devices and the central parameter server. In this paper, we systematically classify gradient and model compression schemes into three categories based on the type of correlations they exploit: structural, temporal, and spatial. We examine the sources of such correlations, propose quantitative metrics for measuring their magnitude, and reinterpret existing compression methods through this unified correlation-based framework. Our experimental studies demonstrate that the degrees of structural, temporal, and spatial correlations vary significantly depending on task complexity, model architecture, and algorithmic configurations. These findings suggest that algorithm designers should carefully evaluate correlation assumptions under specific deployment scenarios rather than assuming that they are always present. Motivated by these findings, we propose two adaptive compression designs that actively switch between different compression modes based on the measured correlation strength, and we evaluate their performance gains relative to conventional non-adaptive approaches. In summary, our unified taxonomy provides a clean and principled foundation for developing more effective and application-specific compression techniques for FL systems.

AI.tldr: A unified framework for FL compression techniques based on three correlation types (structural, temporal, spatial), with adaptive designs that switch compression modes based on measured correlation strength.
AI.motivation: Address the communication bottleneck in federated learning by providing a systematic classification of compression techniques and developing adaptive approaches that account for varying correlation patterns in different deployment scenarios.
AI.method: Classify gradient/model compression into three correlation categories (structural, temporal, spatial), propose metrics to measure correlation magnitude, reinterpret existing methods through this framework, and design adaptive compression that switches modes based on measured correlation strength.
AI.result: Experimental studies show correlation degrees vary significantly with task complexity, model architecture, and algorithmic configurations. Adaptive compression designs outperform conventional non-adaptive approaches by actively switching compression modes based on correlation measurements.
AI.conclusion: The unified taxonomy provides a principled foundation for developing more effective, application-specific compression techniques for FL systems, emphasizing the need to evaluate correlation assumptions rather than assuming they're always present.

title: SatBLIP: Context Understanding and Feature Identification from Satellite Imagery with Vision-Language Learning
summary: Rural environmental risks are shaped by place-based conditions (e.g., housing quality, road access, land-surface patterns), yet standard vulnerability indices are coarse and provide limited insight into risk contexts. We propose SatBLIP, a satellite-specific vision-language framework for rural context understanding and feature identification that predicts county-level Social Vulnerability Index (SVI). SatBLIP addresses limitations of prior remote sensing pipelines-handcrafted features, manual virtual audits, and natural-image-trained VLMs-by coupling contrastive image-text alignment with bootstrapped captioning tailored to satellite semantics. We use GPT-4o to generate structured descriptions of satellite tiles (roof type/condition, house size, yard attributes, greenery, and road context), then fine-tune a satellite-adapted BLIP model to generate captions for unseen images. Captions are encoded with CLIP and fused with LLM-derived embeddings via attention for SVI estimation under spatial aggregation. Using SHAP, we identify salient attributes (e.g., roof form/condition, street width, vegetation, cars/open space) that consistently drive robust predictions, enabling interpretable mapping of rural risk environments.

AI.tldr: SatBLIP: A satellite vision-language framework that predicts county-level Social Vulnerability Index by generating structured descriptions of rural satellite imagery and fusing them with LLM embeddings for interpretable risk mapping.
AI.motivation: Standard vulnerability indices are too coarse for rural areas where place-based conditions (housing quality, road access, land patterns) shape environmental risks. Existing remote sensing approaches have limitations: handcrafted features, manual virtual audits, and natural-image-trained vision-language models don't capture satellite semantics well.
AI.method: SatBLIP couples contrastive image-text alignment with bootstrapped captioning tailored to satellite semantics. Uses GPT-4o to generate structured descriptions of satellite tiles (roof type/condition, house size, yard attributes, greenery, road context), then fine-tunes a satellite-adapted BLIP model to generate captions for unseen images. Captions are encoded with CLIP and fused with LLM-derived embeddings via attention for SVI estimation under spatial aggregation.
AI.result: Using SHAP analysis, the framework identifies salient attributes (roof form/condition, street width, vegetation, cars/open space) that consistently drive robust predictions, enabling interpretable mapping of rural risk environments.
AI.conclusion: SatBLIP provides a novel satellite-specific vision-language approach for rural context understanding that overcomes limitations of prior remote sensing pipelines, offering interpretable feature identification for vulnerability assessment.

title: ProtoAoA: Few-Shot Angle-of-Arrival Estimation using Prototypical Networks
summary: Angle-of-arrival (AoA) estimation is a crucial function in wireless communications used for localization, beam-forming, interference management, and other applications. Deep learning (DL) solutions have been proposed for AoA to mitigate limitations of traditional AoA estimation techniques such as sensitivity to noise and the inability to generalize across different array characteristics. A challenge, however, of DL-based approaches is their reliance on large data collection campaigns and model training. This paper proposes the application of Prototypical Networks (PN) to address this challenge and utilizes a real-world dataset collected on a software defined radio (SDR) testbed to validate the effectiveness of the proposed solution. Prototypical Networks excel in extracting representative embeddings from unstructured input data, establishing class prototypes during training that can be few-shot trained on unseen classes. We demonstrate the efficacy of PNs for AoA classification using complex IQ samples, focusing on its ability to correctly classify new, unseen angles that the model was not trained on previously. Our results show that training our proposed ProtoAoA on only 23% of the AoA dataset classes can attain a mean absolute error (MAE) of 3 degrees with only 4-shots of training on the unseen angles - and an MAE of 2 degrees with 32-shots of training data. These results demonstrate that the developed prototypical network architecture requires remarkably few data samples to achieve reliable AoA estimation - and highlights its potential for other wireless applications where data availability is limited.

AI.tldr: ProtoAoA uses Prototypical Networks for few-shot AoA estimation, achieving 3° MAE with only 23% training data and 4-shot learning on unseen angles.
AI.motivation: Traditional AoA estimation techniques suffer from noise sensitivity and poor generalization across array characteristics. DL approaches require extensive data collection and training, which is impractical in many real-world scenarios.
AI.method: Proposes ProtoAoA using Prototypical Networks that extract representative embeddings from complex IQ samples. The method establishes class prototypes during training and enables few-shot learning on unseen AoA classes with minimal data.
AI.result: Training on only 23% of AoA dataset classes achieves MAE of 3° with 4-shot training on unseen angles, and 2° MAE with 32-shot training. Validated on real-world SDR testbed data.
AI.conclusion: Prototypical Networks enable reliable AoA estimation with remarkably few data samples, demonstrating potential for wireless applications with limited data availability.

title: A Nonasymptotic Theory of Gain-Dependent Error Dynamics in Behavior Cloning
summary: Behavior cloning (BC) policies on position-controlled robots inherit the closed-loop response of the underlying PD controller, yet the effect of controller gains on BC failure lacks a nonasymptotic theory. We show that independent sub-Gaussian action errors propagate through the gain-dependent closed-loop dynamics to yield sub-Gaussian position errors whose proxy matrix $X_\infty(K)$ governs the failure tail. The probability of horizon-$T$ task failure factorizes into a gain-dependent amplification index $Γ_T(K)$ and the validation loss plus a generalization slack, so training loss alone cannot predict closed-loop performance. Under shape-preserving upper-bound structural assumptions the proxy admits the scalar bound $X_\infty(K)\preceqΨ(K)\bar X$ with $Ψ(K)$ decomposed into label difficulty, injection strength, and contraction, ranking the four canonical regimes with compliant-overdamped (CO) tightest, stiff-underdamped (SU) loosest, and the stiff-overdamped versus compliant-underdamped ordering system-dependent. For the canonical scalar second-order PD system the closed-form continuous-time stationary variance $X_\infty^{\mathrm{c}}(α,β)=σ^2α/(2β)$ is strictly monotone in stiffness and damping over the entire stable orthant, covering both underdamped and overdamped regimes, and the exact zero-order-hold (ZOH) discretization inherits this monotonicity. The analysis provides the first nonasymptotic explanation of the empirical finding that compliant, overdamped controllers improve BC success rates.

AI.tldr: BC policies on PD-controlled robots: controller gains affect failure probability via closed-loop dynamics. Failure probability factorizes into gain-dependent amplification and validation loss. Compliant-overdamped controllers yield tightest bounds, explaining empirical BC success improvements.
AI.motivation: Lack of nonasymptotic theory explaining how PD controller gains affect behavior cloning failure rates on position-controlled robots, despite empirical evidence that compliant-overdamped controllers improve BC success.
AI.method: Analyze propagation of sub-Gaussian action errors through gain-dependent closed-loop dynamics to derive position error proxy matrix X∞(K). Factorize horizon-T failure probability into gain-dependent amplification index ΓT(K) and validation loss plus generalization slack. Derive scalar bounds under structural assumptions.
AI.result: Failure probability depends on both training loss and controller gains via amplification index. Four canonical regimes ranked: compliant-overdamped (CO) tightest, stiff-underdamped (SU) loosest. For scalar second-order PD system, closed-form stationary variance X∞c(α,β)=σ²α/(2β) monotonic in stiffness and damping over stable orthant.
AI.conclusion: Provides first nonasymptotic explanation for empirical finding that compliant, overdamped controllers improve BC success rates. Training loss alone cannot predict closed-loop performance - controller gains critically affect failure probability through closed-loop dynamics.

title: Explainable Graph Neural Networks for Interbank Contagion Surveillance: A Regulatory-Aligned Framework for the U.S. Banking Sector
comment: 28 pages, submitted to Research in International Business and Finance (RIBAF)
summary: The Spatial-Temporal Graph Attention Network (ST-GAT) framework was created to serve as an explainable GNN-based solution for detecting bank distress early warning signs and for conducting macro-prudential surveillance of the interbank system in the United States. The ST-GAT framework models 8,103 FDIC insured institutions across 58 quarterly snapshots (2010Q1-2024Q2). Bilateral exposures were reconstructed from publicly available FDIC Call Reports using maximum entropy estimation to produce a dynamic directed weighted graph. The framework achieves the highest AUPRC among all GNN architectures (0.939 +/- 0.010), trailing only XGBoost (0.944). Ablation analysis confirms the BiLSTM temporal component contributes +0.020 AUPRC; temporal attention weights exhibit a monotonically decreasing pattern consistent with long-run structural vulnerability weighting. Permutation importance identifies ROA (0.309) and NPL Ratio (0.252) as dominant predictors, consistent with post-mortem analyses of the 2023 regional banking crisis. All data are publicly available FDIC Call Reports and FRED series; all code and results are released.

AI.tldr: ST-GAT framework uses explainable graph neural networks for early warning of bank distress and macro-prudential surveillance, achieving near-state-of-the-art performance on US interbank data.
AI.motivation: Need for explainable GNN-based solutions for early detection of bank distress and macro-prudential surveillance of the US interbank system, addressing limitations of black-box models.
AI.method: Spatial-Temporal Graph Attention Network (ST-GAT) modeling 8,103 FDIC institutions across 58 quarters (2010-2024), with bilateral exposures reconstructed using maximum entropy estimation from FDIC Call Reports to create dynamic directed weighted graphs.
AI.result: Achieves highest AUPRC among GNN architectures (0.939 ± 0.010), second only to XGBoost (0.944). BiLSTM temporal component adds +0.020 AUPRC; temporal attention shows decreasing pattern; ROA (0.309) and NPL Ratio (0.252) identified as top predictors.
AI.conclusion: ST-GAT provides effective explainable framework for bank distress prediction, with interpretable components and publicly available data/code, validated against 2023 banking crisis patterns.

title: GFT: From Imitation to Reward Fine-Tuning with Unbiased Group Advantages and Dynamic Coefficient Rectification
summary: Large language models are typically post-trained using supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning (RL), yet effectively unifying efficient knowledge injection with robust generalization remains challenging. In this work, we provide a training-dynamics analysis showing that SFT can be interpreted as a special case of policy gradient optimization with an extremely sparse implicit reward and unstable inverse-probability weighting, which together lead to single-path dependency, entropy collapse, and gradient explosion. Motivated by this diagnosis, we propose Group Fine-Tuning (GFT), a unified post-training framework that addresses these intrinsic limitations through two mechanisms: Group Advantage Learning, which constructs diverse response groups and derives normalized contrastive supervision to alleviate reward sparsity, and Dynamic Coefficient Rectification, which adaptively bounds inverse-probability weights to stabilize optimization while preserving efficient knowledge injection. Experiments demonstrate that GFT consistently surpasses SFT-based methods and yields policies that integrate more smoothly with subsequent RL training.

AI.tldr: GFT is a unified post-training framework that addresses SFT's limitations by using group advantage learning and dynamic coefficient rectification to improve training stability and generalization.
AI.motivation: Current LLM post-training methods (SFT and RL) struggle to unify efficient knowledge injection with robust generalization. Analysis shows SFT suffers from sparse implicit rewards, unstable inverse-probability weighting, leading to single-path dependency, entropy collapse, and gradient explosion.
AI.method: Group Fine-Tuning (GFT) with two key mechanisms: 1) Group Advantage Learning - constructs diverse response groups and derives normalized contrastive supervision to alleviate reward sparsity, 2) Dynamic Coefficient Rectification - adaptively bounds inverse-probability weights to stabilize optimization while preserving efficient knowledge injection.
AI.result: GFT consistently surpasses SFT-based methods and yields policies that integrate more smoothly with subsequent RL training.
AI.conclusion: GFT provides a unified framework that addresses intrinsic limitations of SFT, offering improved training stability and better integration with RL, bridging the gap between efficient knowledge injection and robust generalization.

title: Matched and Euclidean-Mismatched Decoding on Fourier-Curve Constellations with Tangent Noise
summary: We study matched and Euclidean-mismatched decoding on finite Fourier-curve constellations with tangent-space artificial noise. Each hypothesis induces a Gaussian law with symbol-dependent rank-one covariance. We derive exact Euclidean pairwise errors for arbitrary pairs and an exact Gaussian-expectation representation for matched decoding on bilaterally tangent-orthogonal pairs. For uniform even constellations, the Euclidean side yields explicit distance spectra and symbol-error bounds across all offset classes; the matched side is exact on antipodal pairs and benchmarked numerically at the full-codebook level via Monte Carlo. By isolating the detection-theoretic consequence of tangent-space artificial noise, these results clarify analytically how noise fraction and constellation density enter the mismatch behavior; secrecy-rate implications require additional channel and adversary modeling.

AI.tldr: Analysis of matched vs Euclidean-mismatched decoding on Fourier-curve constellations with tangent-space artificial noise, deriving exact error expressions and distance spectra.
AI.motivation: To understand the detection-theoretic effects of tangent-space artificial noise on Fourier-curve constellations, specifically how noise fraction and constellation density affect mismatch behavior between matched and Euclidean decoding.
AI.method: Study matched and Euclidean-mismatched decoding on finite Fourier-curve constellations with tangent-space artificial noise. Derive exact Euclidean pairwise errors for arbitrary pairs and exact Gaussian-expectation representation for matched decoding on bilaterally tangent-orthogonal pairs. For uniform even constellations, compute explicit distance spectra and symbol-error bounds across offset classes.
AI.result: Obtained exact Euclidean pairwise errors for arbitrary pairs, exact Gaussian-expectation representation for matched decoding on specific pairs, explicit distance spectra and symbol-error bounds for uniform even constellations. Numerical benchmarking via Monte Carlo for full-codebook level matched decoding.
AI.conclusion: The analysis clarifies how noise fraction and constellation density affect mismatch behavior between matched and Euclidean decoding. The results isolate the detection-theoretic consequences of tangent-space artificial noise, though secrecy-rate implications require additional channel and adversary modeling.

title: FoodSense: A Multisensory Food Dataset and Benchmark for Predicting Taste, Smell, Texture, and Sound from Images
summary: Humans routinely infer taste, smell, texture, and even sound from food images a phenomenon well studied in cognitive science. However, prior vision language research on food has focused primarily on recognition tasks such as meal identification, ingredient detection, and nutrition estimation. Image-based prediction of multisensory experience remains largely unexplored. We introduce FoodSense, a human-annotated dataset for cross-sensory inference containing 66,842 participant-image pairs across 2,987 unique food images. Each pair includes numeric ratings (1-5) and free-text descriptors for four sensory dimensions: taste, smell, texture, and sound. To enable models to both predict and explain sensory expectations, we expand short human annotations into image-grounded reasoning traces. A large language model generates visual justifications conditioned on the image, ratings, and descriptors. Using these annotations, we train FoodSense-VL, a vision language benchmark model to produce both multisensory ratings and grounded explanations directly from food images. This work connects cognitive science findings on cross-sensory perception with modern instruction tuning for multimodal models and shows that many popular evaluation metrics are insufficient for visually sensory inference.

AI.tldr: FoodSense introduces a dataset and benchmark model for predicting multisensory experiences (taste, smell, texture, sound) from food images, bridging cognitive science with multimodal AI.
AI.motivation: While humans naturally infer multisensory experiences from food images, prior computer vision research has focused only on recognition tasks like meal identification and nutrition estimation. The gap between human cross-sensory perception and current AI capabilities motivated this work.
AI.method: Created FoodSense dataset with 66,842 human annotations across 2,987 food images, covering four sensory dimensions with numeric ratings and free-text descriptors. Used LLMs to expand annotations into image-grounded reasoning traces, then trained FoodSense-VL vision-language model for both multisensory prediction and explanation generation.
AI.result: Developed a comprehensive dataset and benchmark model that can predict taste, smell, texture, and sound from food images while providing grounded explanations. Showed that traditional evaluation metrics are insufficient for assessing visual sensory inference tasks.
AI.conclusion: This work successfully bridges cognitive science findings on cross-sensory perception with modern multimodal AI, establishing a foundation for image-based multisensory prediction and highlighting the need for specialized evaluation metrics in this domain.

title: Bridging Standardized Codebook and Site-Specific Beamforming: A Unified Limited-Feedback Framework
summary: A site-specific Type-II codebook design is proposed for downlink massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) limited-feedback beamforming. The key idea is to embed a learned site-specific propagation prior into the Type-II channel state information (CSI) feedback pipeline. Specifically, the base station (BS) uses a low-overhead reference signal received power (RSRP) fingerprint collected during synchronization signal block (SSB) probing to infer a user equipment (UE)-dependent dominant beam subspace before explicit CSI acquisition. The UE then estimates and feeds back only the low-dimensional effective channel coefficients within this inferred subspace, thereby avoiding full-dimensional online subspace discovery while retaining a rich multi-beam representation capability. To analyze the proposed design and compare it with standardized feedback mechanisms, a unified subspace-projection framework is developed by jointly characterizing CSI acquisition, UE-side compression, BS-side reconstruction, and effective spectral efficiency. Under this framework, Type-I, Type-II, port-selection feedback, and the proposed scheme are interpreted as different ways of inducing a feedback representation subspace. The probing codebook and the BS-side subspace inference network are then formulated as a coupled task-oriented design problem and are optimized end-to-end by maximizing the normalized CSI-capture efficiency. Extensive simulation results demonstrate that the proposed feedback scheme achieves Type-II-comparable CSI-capture capability with substantially lower online overhead and UE-side complexity, thereby improving the effective spectral efficiency.

AI.tldr: Proposes site-specific Type-II codebook design for massive MIMO limited-feedback beamforming that embeds learned propagation priors to reduce online overhead while maintaining multi-beam capability.
AI.motivation: Standardized Type-II feedback in massive MIMO systems requires high online overhead and UE-side complexity for full-dimensional subspace discovery. Need to reduce feedback overhead while maintaining rich multi-beam representation capability.
AI.method: Embed site-specific propagation prior into Type-II feedback pipeline using RSRP fingerprints from SSB probing. BS infers UE-dependent dominant beam subspace before explicit CSI acquisition. UE feeds back only low-dimensional coefficients within inferred subspace. Formulate coupled task-oriented design problem for probing codebook and subspace inference network, optimized end-to-end for CSI-capture efficiency.
AI.result: Achieves Type-II-comparable CSI-capture capability with substantially lower online overhead and UE-side complexity. Improves effective spectral efficiency compared to standardized feedback mechanisms.
AI.conclusion: Site-specific Type-II codebook design successfully reduces feedback overhead by leveraging learned propagation priors while maintaining multi-beam representation capability, offering practical improvement for massive MIMO limited-feedback systems.

title: CT-VIR: Continuous-Time Visual-Inertial-Ranging Fusion for Indoor Localization with Sparse Anchors
summary: Visual-inertial odometry (VIO) is widely used for mobile robot localization, but its long-term accuracy degrades without global constraints. Incorporating ranging sensors such as ultra-wideband (UWB) can mitigate drift; however, high-accuracy ranging usually requires well-deployed anchors, which is difficult to ensure in narrow or low-power environments. Moreover, most existing visual-inertial-ranging (VIR) fusion methods rely on discrete time-based filtering or optimization, making it difficult to balance positioning accuracy, trajectory consistency, and fusion efficiency under asynchronous multi-sensor sampling. To address these issues, we propose a spline-based continuous-time state estimation method for VIR fusion localization. In the preprocessing stage, VIO motion priors and UWB ranging measurements are used to construct virtual anchors and reject outliers, thereby alleviating geometric degeneration and improving range reliability. In the estimation stage, the pose trajectory is parameterized in continuous time using a B-spline, while inertial, visual, and ranging constraints are formulated as factors in a sliding-window graph. The spline control points, together with a small set of auxiliary parameters, are then jointly optimized to obtain a continuous-time trajectory estimate. Evaluations on public datasets and real-world experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and practical potential of the proposed approach.

AI.tldr: A continuous-time spline-based method for visual-inertial-ranging fusion that uses B-splines to handle asynchronous sensor data, improves UWB reliability through virtual anchors, and achieves better accuracy than discrete-time approaches.
AI.motivation: VIO suffers from drift without global constraints, UWB requires well-deployed anchors which is difficult in narrow/low-power environments, and existing discrete-time VIR fusion methods struggle with balancing accuracy, consistency, and efficiency under asynchronous multi-sensor sampling.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: 1) Preprocessing uses VIO motion priors and UWB ranging to construct virtual anchors and reject outliers; 2) Estimation parameterizes pose trajectory with B-splines in continuous time, formulates inertial, visual, and ranging constraints as factors in sliding-window graph, and jointly optimizes spline control points with auxiliary parameters.
AI.result: Evaluations on public datasets and real-world experiments demonstrate effectiveness and practical potential of the proposed approach, showing improved positioning accuracy and trajectory consistency.
AI.conclusion: The spline-based continuous-time state estimation method effectively addresses limitations of discrete-time VIR fusion, providing better handling of asynchronous sensor data, improved UWB reliability through virtual anchors, and superior positioning performance.

title: Graph-Based Fraud Detection with Dual-Path Graph Filtering
summary: Fraud detection on graph data can be viewed as a demanding task that requires distinguishing between different types of nodes. Because graph neural networks (GNNs) are naturally suited for processing information encoded in graph form through their message-passing operations, methods based on GNN models have increasingly attracted attention in the fraud detection domain. However, fraud graphs inherently exhibit relation camouflage, high heterophily, and class imbalance, causing most GNNs to underperform in fraud detection tasks. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a Graph-Based Fraud Detection Model with Dual-Path Graph Filtering (DPF-GFD). DPF-GFD first applies a beta wavelet-based operator to the original graph to capture key structural patterns. It then constructs a similarity graph from distance-based node representations and applies an improved low-pass filter. The embeddings from the original and similarity graphs are fused through supervised representation learning to obtain node features, which are finally used by an ensemble tree model to assess the fraud risk of unlabeled nodes. Unlike existing single-graph smoothing approaches, DPF-GFD introduces a frequency-complementary dual-path filtering paradigm tailored for fraud detection, explicitly decoupling structural anomaly modeling and feature similarity modeling. This design enables more discriminative and stable node representations in highly heterophilous and imbalanced fraud graphs. Comprehensive experiments on four real-world financial fraud detection datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed method.

AI.tldr: DPF-GFD is a graph-based fraud detection model using dual-path graph filtering to handle relation camouflage, high heterophily, and class imbalance in fraud graphs.
AI.motivation: Fraud detection on graphs faces challenges due to relation camouflage, high heterophily, and class imbalance, causing most GNNs to underperform. Existing single-graph smoothing approaches are insufficient for these complex fraud detection scenarios.
AI.method: Proposes DPF-GFD with dual-path graph filtering: 1) Beta wavelet-based operator on original graph to capture structural patterns, 2) Constructs similarity graph from distance-based node representations with improved low-pass filter, 3) Fuses embeddings from both graphs through supervised representation learning, 4) Uses ensemble tree model for final fraud risk assessment.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments on four real-world financial fraud detection datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of DPF-GFD, showing improved performance in handling heterophilous and imbalanced fraud graphs.
AI.conclusion: DPF-GFD introduces a frequency-complementary dual-path filtering paradigm that explicitly decouples structural anomaly modeling and feature similarity modeling, enabling more discriminative and stable node representations for fraud detection in challenging graph environments.

title: Seeing Through Experts Eyes A Foundational Vision Language Model Trained on Radiologists Gaze and Reasoning
summary: Large scale vision language models have shown promise in automating chest Xray interpretation, yet their clinical utility remains limited by a gap between model outputs and radiologist reasoning. Most systems optimize for semantic information without emulating how experts visually examine medical images, often overlooking critical findings or diverging from established diagnostic workflows. Radiologists follow structured protocols (e.g., the ABCDEF approach) that ensure all clinically relevant regions are systematically examined, reducing missed findings and supporting reliable diagnostic reasoning. We introduce GazeX, a vision language model that leverages radiologists' eye tracking data as a behavioral prior to model expert diagnostic reasoning. By incorporating gaze trajectories and fixation patterns into pretraining, GazeX learns to follow the spatial and temporal structure of radiologist attention and integrates observations in a clinically meaningful sequence. Using a curated dataset of over 30,000 gaze key frames from five radiologists, we demonstrate that GazeX produces more accurate, interpretable, and expert consistent outputs across radiology report generation, disease grounding, and visual question answering, utilizing 231,835 radiographic studies, 780,014 question answer pairs, and 1,162 image sentence pairs with bounding boxes. Unlike autonomous reporting systems, GazeX produces verifiable evidence artifacts, including inspection trajectories and finding linked localized regions, enabling efficient human verification and safe human AI collaboration. Learning through expert eyes provides a practical route toward more trustworthy, explainable, and diagnostically robust AI systems for radiology and beyond.

AI.tldr: GazeX is a vision-language model that uses radiologists' eye-tracking data to emulate expert diagnostic reasoning, improving accuracy and interpretability in chest X-ray analysis.
AI.motivation: Current vision-language models for chest X-ray interpretation lack clinical utility because they don't emulate how radiologists visually examine images. They miss critical findings and diverge from established diagnostic workflows that use structured protocols like ABCDEF.
AI.method: GazeX incorporates radiologists' gaze trajectories and fixation patterns into pretraining, learning to follow the spatial and temporal structure of expert attention. Uses a curated dataset of over 30,000 gaze key frames from five radiologists.
AI.result: GazeX produces more accurate, interpretable, and expert-consistent outputs across radiology report generation, disease grounding, and visual question answering. Tested on 231,835 radiographic studies, 780,014 QA pairs, and 1,162 image-sentence pairs with bounding boxes.
AI.conclusion: Learning through expert eyes provides a practical route toward more trustworthy, explainable, and diagnostically robust AI systems for radiology. GazeX produces verifiable evidence artifacts enabling efficient human verification and safe human-AI collaboration.

title: Regret Tail Characterization of Optimal Bandit Algorithms with Generic Rewards
summary:
We study the tail behavior of regret in stochastic multi-armed bandits for algorithms that are asymptotically optimal in expectation. While minimizing expected regret is the classical objective, recent work shows that even such algorithms can exhibit heavy regret tails, incurring large regret with non-negligible probability. Existing sharp characterizations of regret tails are largely restricted to parametric settings, such as single-parameter exponential families.
  In this work, we extend the $\KLinf$-UCB algorithm of to a broad nonparametric class of reward distributions satisfying mild assumptions, and establish its asymptotic optimality in expectation. We then analyze the tail behavior of its regret and derive a novel upper bound on the regret tail probability. As special cases, our results recover regret-tail guarantees for both bounded-support and heavy-tailed (moment-bounded) bandit models. Moreover, for the special case of finitely-supported reward distributions, our upper bound matches the known lower bound exactly. Our results thus provide a unified and tight characterization of regret tails for asymptotically optimal KL-based UCB algorithms, going beyond parametric models.

AI.tldr: The paper extends KLinf-UCB algorithm to nonparametric reward distributions, proves its asymptotic optimality, and provides tight regret tail bounds that match known lower bounds for finitely-supported distributions.
AI.motivation: Existing regret tail characterizations are largely limited to parametric settings, but asymptotically optimal bandit algorithms can still exhibit heavy regret tails. The paper aims to extend these analyses to broader nonparametric distribution classes.
AI.method: Extends the KLinf-UCB algorithm to nonparametric reward distributions satisfying mild assumptions, establishes asymptotic optimality in expectation, and analyzes regret tail behavior using novel upper bounds on regret tail probability.
AI.result: Proves asymptotic optimality of the extended KLinf-UCB algorithm and derives tight regret tail bounds that recover known results for bounded-support and heavy-tailed models, matching lower bounds for finitely-supported distributions.
AI.conclusion: Provides unified and tight characterization of regret tails for asymptotically optimal KL-based UCB algorithms beyond parametric models, with results that apply to both bounded-support and heavy-tailed reward distributions.

title: Zero-Ablation Overstates Register Content Dependence in DINO Vision Transformers
comment: 12 pages, 10 figures, to be published in CVPR 2026 HOW Vision Interpretability Workshop Proceedings
summary: Zero-ablation -- replacing token activations with zero vectors -- is widely used to probe token function in vision transformers. Register zeroing in DINOv2+registers and DINOv3 produces large drops (up to $-36.6$\,pp classification, $-30.9$\,pp segmentation), suggesting registers are functionally indispensable. However, three replacement controls -- mean-substitution, noise-substitution, and cross-image register-shuffling -- preserve performance across classification, correspondence, and segmentation, remaining within ${\sim}1$\,pp of the unmodified baseline. Per-patch cosine similarity shows these replacements genuinely perturb internal representations, while zeroing causes disproportionately large perturbations, consistent with why it alone degrades tasks. We conclude that zero-ablation overstates dependence on exact register content. In the frozen-feature evaluations we test, performance depends on plausible register-like activations rather than on exact image-specific values. Registers nevertheless buffer dense features from \texttt{[CLS]} dependence and are associated with compressed patch geometry. These findings, including the replacement-control results, replicate at ViT-B scale.

AI.tldr: Zero-ablation overstates register importance; replacement controls show performance depends on plausible register-like activations, not exact values.
AI.motivation: To challenge the common practice of zero-ablation for probing token function in vision transformers, particularly for registers in DINOv2/DINOv3, by showing it may overstate dependence on exact register content.
AI.method: Used three replacement controls: mean-substitution, noise-substitution, and cross-image register-shuffling, compared to zero-ablation. Measured performance on classification, correspondence, and segmentation tasks, and analyzed per-patch cosine similarity to assess perturbation magnitude.
AI.result: Zero-ablation causes large performance drops (up to -36.6pp classification, -30.9pp segmentation), but replacement controls preserve performance within ~1pp of baseline. Per-patch cosine similarity shows replacements genuinely perturb representations, but zeroing causes disproportionately large perturbations.
AI.conclusion: Zero-ablation overstates dependence on exact register content; performance depends on plausible register-like activations rather than exact image-specific values. Registers buffer dense features from [CLS] dependence and are associated with compressed patch geometry. Results replicate at ViT-B scale.

title: Beam Squinting Effects in Super Wideband Communication Systems
comment: This paper has 6 pages and 3 figures. This paper has been submitted to an IEEE Conference for possible publication
summary: Beam squint, the frequency-dependent shift of the main beam, poses a major challenge for wideband antenna arrays. This paper focuses on the beam squint effects in super wideband (SW) systems, where high mutual coupling (MC) effects are present. These high MC effects complicate beamforming (BF) by creating frequency-dependent phase relationships that invalidate conventional approaches. To accurately model MC effects, this paper uses a circuit-theoretic framework for tightly coupled SW uniform linear arrays (ULAs). We derive closed-form expressions for the average received signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) with BF in conventional half-wavelength spaced, weakly coupled arrays and validate them. Extending our analysis to tightly coupled SW arrays, we demonstrate that, in contrast to conventional weakly coupled arrays, the effective true time delays exhibit a nonlinear dependence on frequency due to coupling-induced phase shifts. A comparative analysis reveals that strong MC in SW arrays significantly reduces squint in phase-controlled BF, extending the usable bandwidth considerably.

AI.tldr: Beam squint in super wideband arrays is reduced by strong mutual coupling, which creates nonlinear frequency-dependent phase shifts that extend usable bandwidth.
AI.motivation: Beam squint (frequency-dependent beam shift) is a major challenge for wideband antenna arrays, especially in super wideband systems with high mutual coupling effects that complicate beamforming by creating frequency-dependent phase relationships.
AI.method: Uses circuit-theoretic framework for tightly coupled super wideband uniform linear arrays, derives closed-form expressions for average received SNR with beamforming in conventional arrays, and extends analysis to tightly coupled arrays to study coupling-induced phase shifts.
AI.result: Strong mutual coupling in super wideband arrays significantly reduces squint in phase-controlled beamforming, extending the usable bandwidth considerably. The effective true time delays exhibit nonlinear dependence on frequency due to coupling-induced phase shifts.
AI.conclusion: Mutual coupling effects in super wideband arrays, while traditionally considered problematic, actually help mitigate beam squint and extend usable bandwidth through nonlinear frequency-dependent phase relationships.

title: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning Exploits Passive Body Dynamics for High-Performance Biped Robot Locomotion
summary: Embodiment is a significant keyword in recent machine learning fields. This study focused on the passive nature of the body of a biped robot to generate walking and running locomotion using model-based deep reinforcement learning. We constructed two models in a simulator, one with passive elements (e.g., springs) and the other, which is similar to general humanoids, without passive elements. The training of the model with passive elements was highly affected by the attractor of the system. This lead that although the trajectories quickly converged to limit cycles, it took a long time to obtain large rewards. However, thanks to the attractor-driven learning, the acquired locomotion was robust and energy-efficient. The results revealed that robots with passive elements could efficiently acquire high-performance locomotion by utilizing stable limit cycles generated through dynamic interaction between the body and ground. This study demonstrates the importance of implementing passive properties in the body for future embodied AI.

AI.tldr: Using model-based deep reinforcement learning, this study shows that biped robots with passive elements (springs) can learn robust, energy-efficient walking/running by leveraging stable limit cycles from body-ground interaction.
AI.motivation: To explore how passive body properties (embodiment) in biped robots can facilitate learning of walking and running locomotion through model-based deep reinforcement learning, comparing robots with and without passive elements.
AI.method: Constructed two simulated biped robot models: one with passive elements (springs) and one without (similar to general humanoids). Used model-based deep reinforcement learning to train both models for walking and running locomotion.
AI.result: The passive-element robot's training was highly affected by system attractors - trajectories quickly converged to limit cycles but took longer to achieve high rewards. However, the resulting locomotion was more robust and energy-efficient compared to the non-passive model.
AI.conclusion: Robots with passive elements can efficiently acquire high-performance locomotion by utilizing stable limit cycles from dynamic body-ground interaction, demonstrating the importance of implementing passive properties in robot bodies for future embodied AI.

title: TOPCELL: Topology Optimization of Standard Cell via LLMs
comment: Accepted to the 63rd ACM/IEEE Design Automation Conference (DAC 2026). 7 pages, 4 figures
summary: Transistor topology optimization is a critical step in standard cell design, directly dictating diffusion sharing efficiency and downstream routability. However, identifying optimal topologies remains a persistent bottleneck, as conventional exhaustive search methods become computationally intractable with increasing circuit complexity in advanced nodes. This paper introduces TOPCELL, a novel and scalable framework that reformulates high-dimensional topology exploration as a generative task using Large Language Models (LLMs). We employ Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to fine-tune the model, aligning its topology optimization strategy with logical (circuit) and spatial (layout) constraints. Experimental results within an industrial flow targeting an advanced 2nm technology node demonstrate that TOPCELL significantly outperforms foundation models in discovering routable, physically-aware topologies. When integrated into a state-of-the-art (SOTA) automation flow for a 7nm library generation task, TOPCELL exhibits robust zero-shot generalization and matches the layout quality of exhaustive solvers while achieving an 85.91x speedup.

AI.tldr: TOPCELL uses LLMs with GRPO fine-tuning to optimize transistor topologies for standard cell design, achieving 85.91x speedup over exhaustive methods while matching layout quality.
AI.motivation: Transistor topology optimization is crucial for standard cell design but conventional exhaustive search methods become computationally intractable with increasing circuit complexity in advanced nodes, creating a persistent bottleneck.
AI.method: Reformulates high-dimensional topology exploration as a generative task using Large Language Models (LLMs) with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) fine-tuning to align with logical (circuit) and spatial (layout) constraints.
AI.result: Significantly outperforms foundation models in discovering routable, physically-aware topologies for 2nm technology. Achieves 85.91x speedup over exhaustive solvers while matching layout quality in 7nm library generation with robust zero-shot generalization.
AI.conclusion: TOPCELL provides a scalable framework for transistor topology optimization that overcomes computational limitations of conventional methods while maintaining high layout quality through LLM-based generative approach with GRPO fine-tuning.

title: Mistake gating leads to energy and memory efficient continual learning
summary: Synaptic plasticity is metabolically expensive, yet animals continuously update their internal models without exhausting energy reserves. However, when artificial neural networks are trained, the network parameters are typically updated on every sample that is presented, even if the sample was classified correctly. Inspired by the human negativity bias and error-related negativity, we propose 'memorized mistake-gated learning' -- a biologically plausible plasticity rule where synaptic updates are strictly gated by current and past classification errors. This reduces the number of updates the network needs to make by $50\%\sim80\%$. Mistake gating is particularly well suited in two cases: 1) For incremental learning where new knowledge is acquired on a background of pre-existing knowledge, 2) For online learning scenarios when data needs to be stored for later replay, as mistake-gating reduces storage buffer requirements. The algorithm can be implemented in a few lines of code, adds no hyper-parameters, and comes at negligible computational overhead. Learning on mistakes is an energy efficient and biologically relevant modification to commonly used learning rules that is well suited for continual learning.

AI.tldr: A biologically-inspired learning rule called "memorized mistake-gated learning" reduces synaptic updates by 50-80% by only updating weights when classification errors occur, improving energy efficiency for continual learning.
AI.motivation: The paper is motivated by the observation that biological synaptic plasticity is metabolically expensive, yet animals continuously learn without exhausting energy reserves. In contrast, artificial neural networks typically update parameters on every sample, even when correctly classified, which is inefficient. The authors draw inspiration from human negativity bias and error-related negativity in biological systems.
AI.method: The authors propose "memorized mistake-gated learning" - a biologically plausible plasticity rule where synaptic updates are strictly gated by current and past classification errors. The algorithm tracks mistakes and only performs weight updates when errors occur, reducing unnecessary computations. It can be implemented with minimal code, adds no hyperparameters, and has negligible computational overhead.
AI.result: The method reduces the number of network updates needed by 50-80%. It is particularly effective for incremental learning (acquiring new knowledge on pre-existing knowledge) and online learning scenarios where data needs to be stored for replay, as it reduces storage buffer requirements.
AI.conclusion: Learning on mistakes is an energy-efficient and biologically relevant modification to common learning rules that is well-suited for continual learning. The approach offers practical benefits for real-world applications where computational efficiency and energy conservation are important considerations.

title: Support Size of $\varepsilon$-Capacity-Achieving Inputs for the Amplitude-Constrained AWGN Channel
comment: Extended version of a paper submitted to IEEE ITW 2026
summary: We study the amplitude-constrained additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN) channel from the perspective of near-optimal input distributions. While it is known that the capacity-achieving input is discrete with finitely many mass points, the precise scaling of its support size as a function of the amplitude constraint remains an open problem. In this work, we instead consider the minimal support size required to achieve capacity up to an $\varepsilon$-gap. We introduce the quantity $K_\varepsilon(A)$, defined as the smallest support size among discrete inputs supported on $[-A,A]$ that achieves mutual information within $\varepsilon$ of capacity. We show that this relaxed formulation is significantly more tractable and admits sharp characterizations across different regimes of $\varepsilon$. In particular, when $\varepsilon$ decays polynomially with $A$, i.e., $\varepsilon = A^{-β}$ for $β\geq 1$, we establish that $K_\varepsilon(A) = Θ(A\sqrt{\log A})$. For exponentially small gaps, we obtain bounds of order between $A\sqrt{\log A}$ and $A^{3/2}$. Our approach combines approximation-theoretic bounds for Gaussian mixtures with information-theoretic control of entropy via $χ^2$-divergence, together with a wrapping argument that relates the problem to approximating the uniform distribution on the circle. Beyond the technical results, our framework provides a conceptual explanation for the variety of scaling laws observed in prior numerical studies, showing that these correspond to different regimes of $\varepsilon$-optimality rather than intrinsic properties of the exact optimizer.

AI.tldr: The paper studies near-optimal input distributions for amplitude-constrained AWGN channels, focusing on minimal support size needed to achieve capacity within ε-gap, showing different scaling laws for polynomial vs exponential ε decay.
AI.motivation: While capacity-achieving input for amplitude-constrained AWGN channels is known to be discrete with finite support, the exact scaling of support size with amplitude constraint A remains open. The paper addresses this by studying a more tractable ε-approximation problem.
AI.method: Introduces K_ε(A) as minimal support size among discrete inputs on [-A,A] achieving mutual information within ε of capacity. Combines approximation-theoretic bounds for Gaussian mixtures with information-theoretic control via χ²-divergence, and a wrapping argument relating to approximating uniform distribution on circle.
AI.result: For polynomial ε decay (ε = A^{-β}, β≥1): K_ε(A) = Θ(A√log A). For exponentially small gaps: bounds between A√log A and A^{3/2}. Provides conceptual explanation for scaling laws observed in prior numerical studies.
AI.conclusion: The ε-optimal formulation is more tractable than exact capacity-achieving distribution analysis, revealing different scaling regimes based on approximation accuracy. Prior numerical observations correspond to different ε-optimality regimes rather than intrinsic properties of exact optimizer.

title: Crowdsourcing of Real-world Image Annotation via Visual Properties
summary: Recent advances in data-centric artificial intelligence highlight inherent limitations in object recognition datasets. One of the primary issues stems from the semantic gap problem, which results in complex many-to-many mappings between visual data and linguistic descriptions. This bias adversely affects performance in computer vision tasks. This paper proposes an image annotation methodology that integrates knowledge representation, natural language processing, and computer vision techniques, aiming to reduce annotator subjectivity by applying visual property constraints. We introduce an interactive crowdsourcing framework that dynamically asks questions based on a predefined object category hierarchy and annotator feedback, guiding image annotation by visual properties. Experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of this methodology, and annotator feedback is discussed to optimize the crowdsourcing setup.

AI.tldr: Proposes an interactive crowdsourcing framework for image annotation that uses visual property constraints and dynamic questioning to reduce semantic gap and annotator subjectivity.
AI.motivation: Addresses limitations in object recognition datasets caused by semantic gap problems (many-to-many mappings between visual data and linguistic descriptions) and annotator subjectivity bias that affects computer vision performance.
AI.method: Integrates knowledge representation, NLP, and computer vision techniques. Uses an interactive crowdsourcing framework that dynamically asks questions based on predefined object category hierarchy and annotator feedback, guiding annotation by visual properties.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of the methodology. Annotator feedback is discussed to optimize the crowdsourcing setup.
AI.conclusion: The proposed image annotation methodology successfully reduces annotator subjectivity through visual property constraints and interactive guidance, addressing semantic gap issues in object recognition datasets.

title: Low-Complexity Soft-Feedback Detector for AFDM Systems
comment: 5 pages, 5 figures. Accepted to the 2026 IEEE International Symposium on Information Theory (ISIT)
summary: Affine frequency division multiplexing (AFDM), an emerging multi-carrier modulation scheme, has garnered significant attention due to its resilience to Doppler shifts and capability to achieve full diversity in doubly dispersive channels. However, existing data detection algorithms for AFDM systems face a significant trade-off between computational complexity and accuracy. In this paper, a novel low-complexity data detection scheme, termed the soft-feedback detector (SFD), is proposed. Particularly, building upon a maximum ratio combining (MRC) estimator framework, the SFD leverages the a priori symbol distribution to mitigate error propagation during iterative detection. Specifically, soft-decision feedback is incorporated as extrinsic information derived from the log-likelihood ratios of the transmitted symbols. As a result, the proposed detector significantly enhances detection accuracy while maintaining low computational complexity. Simulation results demonstrate that the SFD consistently outperforms benchmark decision-feedback detectors. In particular, compared with the conventional MRC detector, the proposed scheme achieves approximately a 3 dB signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) gain at the bit error rate (BER) of $10^{-3}$.

AI.tldr: A novel soft-feedback detector (SFD) for AFDM systems achieves ~3dB SNR gain at BER 10⁻³ with low complexity by using soft-decision feedback from symbol distributions to reduce error propagation.
AI.motivation: Existing AFDM data detection algorithms face a trade-off between computational complexity and accuracy. AFDM is promising for doubly dispersive channels but needs better detection methods that balance performance and efficiency.
AI.method: Proposes a soft-feedback detector (SFD) based on maximum ratio combining (MRC) estimator framework. Incorporates soft-decision feedback as extrinsic information from log-likelihood ratios of transmitted symbols to mitigate error propagation during iterative detection.
AI.result: SFD consistently outperforms benchmark decision-feedback detectors. Achieves approximately 3 dB SNR gain at BER 10⁻³ compared to conventional MRC detector while maintaining low computational complexity.
AI.conclusion: The proposed SFD significantly enhances detection accuracy for AFDM systems while maintaining low computational complexity, offering a practical solution to the complexity-accuracy trade-off in doubly dispersive channels.

title: A multi-platform LiDAR dataset for standardized forest inventory measurement at long term ecological monitoring sites
summary: We present a curated multi-platform LiDAR reference dataset from an instrumented ICOS forest plot, explicitly designed to support calibration, benchmarking, and integration of 3D structural data with ecological observations and standard allometric models. The dataset integrates UAV-borne laser scanning (ULS) to measure canopy coverage, terrestrial laser scanning (TLS) for detailed stem mapping, and backpack mobile laser scanning (MLS) with real-time SLAM for efficient sub-canopy acquisition. We focus on the control plot with the most complete and internally consistent registration, where TLS point clouds (~333 million points) are complemented by ULS and MLS data capturing canopy and understory strata. Marker-free, SLAM-aware protocols were used to reduce field and processing time, while manual and automated methods were combined. Final products are available in LAZ and E57 formats with UTM coordinates, together with registration reports for reproducibility. The dataset provides a benchmark for testing registration methods, evaluating scanning efficiency, and linking point clouds with segmentation, quantitative structure models, and allometric biomass estimation. By situating the acquisitions at a long-term ICOS site, it is explicitly linked to 3D structure with decades of ecological and flux measurements. More broadly, it illustrates how TLS, MLS, and ULS can be combined for repeated inventories and digital twins of forest ecosystems.

AI.tldr: A multi-platform LiDAR dataset from an ICOS forest plot integrating UAV, terrestrial, and mobile laser scanning for 3D forest structure analysis and ecological integration.
AI.motivation: To create a comprehensive reference dataset that supports calibration, benchmarking, and integration of 3D structural data with ecological observations and standard allometric models for forest ecosystems.
AI.method: Integrated UAV-borne laser scanning (ULS) for canopy coverage, terrestrial laser scanning (TLS) for detailed stem mapping, and backpack mobile laser scanning (MLS) with real-time SLAM for efficient sub-canopy acquisition. Used marker-free, SLAM-aware protocols to reduce field and processing time, combining manual and automated methods.
AI.result: Created a curated dataset with ~333 million TLS points complemented by ULS and MLS data capturing canopy and understory strata. Final products available in LAZ and E57 formats with UTM coordinates and registration reports for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: The dataset provides a benchmark for testing registration methods, evaluating scanning efficiency, and linking point clouds with segmentation, quantitative structure models, and allometric biomass estimation. It demonstrates how TLS, MLS, and ULS can be combined for repeated inventories and digital twins of forest ecosystems, explicitly linking 3D structure with decades of ecological and flux measurements.

title: Optimistic Policy Learning under Pessimistic Adversaries with Regret and Violation Guarantees
summary:
Real-world decision-making systems operate in environments where state transitions depend not only on the agent's actions, but also on \textbf{exogenous factors outside its control}--competing agents, environmental disturbances, or strategic adversaries--formally, $s_{h+1} = f(s_h, a_h, \bar{a}_h)+ω_h$ where $\bar{a}_h$ is the adversary/external action, $a_h$ is the agent's action, and $ω_h$ is an additive noise. Ignoring such factors can yield policies that are optimal in isolation but \textbf{fail catastrophically in deployment}, particularly when safety constraints must be satisfied.
  Standard Constrained MDP formulations assume the agent is the sole driver of state evolution, an assumption that breaks down in safety-critical settings. Existing robust RL approaches address this via distributional robustness over transition kernels, but do not explicitly model the \textbf{strategic interaction} between agent and exogenous factor, and rely on strong assumptions about divergence from a known nominal model.
  We model the exogenous factor as an \textbf{adversarial policy} $\barπ$ that co-determines state transitions, and ask how an agent can remain both optimal and safe against such an adversary. \emph{To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to study safety-constrained RL under explicit adversarial dynamics}. We propose \textbf{Robust Hallucinated Constrained Upper-Confidence RL} (\texttt{RHC-UCRL}), a model-based algorithm that maintains optimism over both agent and adversary policies, explicitly separating epistemic from aleatoric uncertainty. \texttt{RHC-UCRL} achieves sub-linear regret and constraint violation guarantees.

AI.tldr: First work on safety-constrained RL with explicit adversarial dynamics, proposing RHC-UCRL algorithm with sub-linear regret and constraint violation guarantees.
AI.motivation: Real-world decision systems face exogenous factors (competing agents, disturbances, adversaries) that affect state transitions, which standard CMDPs ignore. Ignoring these factors leads to policies that fail catastrophically in deployment, especially for safety-critical applications.
AI.method: Model exogenous factors as adversarial policy that co-determines state transitions. Propose Robust Hallucinated Constrained Upper-Confidence RL (RHC-UCRL), a model-based algorithm that maintains optimism over both agent and adversary policies, explicitly separating epistemic from aleatoric uncertainty.
AI.result: RHC-UCRL achieves sub-linear regret and constraint violation guarantees, addressing safety-constrained RL under explicit adversarial dynamics for the first time.
AI.conclusion: This work pioneers safety-constrained RL with adversarial dynamics, providing theoretical guarantees for robust and safe decision-making in environments with strategic adversaries or exogenous factors.

title: Credo: Declarative Control of LLM Pipelines via Beliefs and Policies
summary: Agentic AI systems are becoming commonplace in domains that require long-lived, stateful decision-making in continuously evolving conditions. As such, correctness depends not only on the output of individual model calls, but also on how to best adapt when incorporating new evidence or revising prior conclusions. However, existing frameworks rely on imperative control loops, ephemeral memory, and prompt-embedded logic, making agent behavior opaque, brittle, and difficult to verify. This paper introduces Credo, which represents semantic state as beliefs and regulates behavior using declarative policies defined over these beliefs. This design supports adaptive, auditable, and composable execution through a database-backed semantic control plane. We showcase these concepts in a decision-control scenario, where beliefs and policies declaratively guide critical execution choices (e.g., model selection, retrieval, corrective re-execution), enabling dynamic behavior without requiring any changes to the underlying pipeline code.
