AI.conclusion: Learning to construct explicit layouts instills spatial understanding that generalizes beyond training format, similar to how sketching sharpens human spatial thinking. The approach bridges the gap between representation construction and reasoning.

title: DETR-ViP: Detection Transformer with Robust Discriminative Visual Prompts
comment: Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2026
summary: Visual prompted object detection enables interactive and flexible definition of target categories, thereby facilitating open-vocabulary detection. Since visual prompts are derived directly from image features, they often outperform text prompts in recognizing rare categories. Nevertheless, research on visual prompted detection has been largely overlooked, and it is typically treated as a byproduct of training text prompted detectors, which hinders its development. To fully unlock the potential of visual-prompted detection, we investigate the reasons why its performance is suboptimal and reveal that the underlying issue lies in the absence of global discriminability in visual prompts. Motivated by these observations, we propose DETR-ViP, a robust object detection framework that yields class-distinguishable visual prompts. On top of basic image-text contrastive learning, DETR-ViP incorporates global prompt integration and visual-textual prompt relation distillation to learn more discriminative prompt representations. In addition, DETR-ViP employs a selective fusion strategy that ensures stable and robust detection. Extensive experiments on COCO, LVIS, ODinW, and Roboflow100 demonstrate that DETR-ViP achieves substantially higher performance in visual prompt detection compared to other state-of-the-art counterparts. A series of ablation studies and analyses further validate the effectiveness of the proposed improvements and shed light on the underlying reasons for the enhanced detection capability of visual prompts.

AI.tldr: DETR-ViP improves visual-prompted object detection by addressing the lack of global discriminability in visual prompts through global prompt integration, visual-textual prompt relation distillation, and selective fusion strategy.
AI.motivation: Visual prompted detection has been overlooked and treated as a byproduct of text prompted detectors, limiting its development. Current visual prompts lack global discriminability, leading to suboptimal performance despite their potential advantages for rare categories.
AI.method: DETR-ViP framework incorporates: 1) Global prompt integration to enhance discriminability, 2) Visual-textual prompt relation distillation to learn more discriminative representations, and 3) Selective fusion strategy for stable and robust detection, building on basic image-text contrastive learning.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on COCO, LVIS, ODinW, and Roboflow100 demonstrate substantially higher performance in visual prompt detection compared to state-of-the-art counterparts. Ablation studies validate the effectiveness of the proposed improvements.
AI.conclusion: DETR-ViP successfully addresses the global discriminability issue in visual prompts, unlocking their full potential for open-vocabulary object detection, particularly for rare categories where visual prompts outperform text prompts.

title: Physics-Informed Machine Learning for Pouch Cell Temperature Estimation
summary: Accurate temperature estimation of pouch cells with indirect liquid cooling is essential for optimizing battery thermal management systems for transportation electrification. However, it is challenging due to the computational expense of finite element simulations and the limitations of data-driven models. This paper presents a physics-informed machine learning (PIML) framework for the efficient and reliable estimation of steady-state temperature profiles. The PIML approach integrates the governing heat transfer equations directly into the neural network's loss function, enabling high-fidelity predictions with significantly faster convergence than purely data-driven methods. The framework is evaluated on a dataset of varying cooling channel geometries. Results demonstrate that the PIML model converges more rapidly and achieves markedly higher accuracy, with a 49.1% reduction in mean squared error over the data-driven model. Validation against independent test cases further confirms its superior performance, particularly in regions away from the cooling channels. These findings underscore the potential of PIML for surrogate modeling and design optimization in battery systems.

AI.tldr: A physics-informed machine learning framework for efficient temperature estimation in pouch cells with indirect liquid cooling, achieving 49.1% lower error than data-driven methods.
AI.motivation: Accurate temperature estimation of pouch cells with indirect liquid cooling is essential for battery thermal management in transportation electrification, but challenging due to computational expense of finite element simulations and limitations of data-driven models.
AI.method: Physics-informed machine learning (PIML) framework that integrates governing heat transfer equations directly into the neural network's loss function for steady-state temperature profile estimation.
AI.result: PIML model converges more rapidly and achieves 49.1% reduction in mean squared error over data-driven model, with superior performance validated against independent test cases, especially in regions away from cooling channels.
AI.conclusion: The findings underscore the potential of physics-informed machine learning for surrogate modeling and design optimization in battery thermal management systems.

title: Targeted Exploration via Unified Entropy Control for Reinforcement Learning
comment: Accepted for publication in Findings of the 64th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (ACL 2026)
summary: Recent advances in reinforcement learning (RL) have improved the reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (VLMs). However, the widely used Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) consistently suffers from entropy collapse, causing the policy to converge prematurely and lose diversity. Existing exploration methods introduce additional bias or variance during exploration, making it difficult to maintain optimization stability. We propose Unified Entropy Control for Reinforcement Learning (UEC-RL), a framework that provides targeted mechanisms for exploration and stabilization. UEC-RL activates more exploration on difficult prompts to search for potential and valuable reasoning trajectories. In parallel, a stabilizer prevents entropy from growing uncontrollably, thereby keeping training stable as the model consolidates reliable behaviors. Together, these components expand the search space when needed while maintaining robust optimization throughout training. Experiments on both LLM and VLM reasoning tasks show consistent gains over RL baselines on both Pass@1 and Pass@$k$. On Geometry3K, UEC-RL achieves a 37.9\% relative improvement over GRPO, indicating that it sustains effective exploration without compromising convergence and underscoring UEC-RL as a key for scaling RL-based reasoning in large models. Our code is available at https://github.com/597358816/UEC-RL.

AI.tldr: UEC-RL is a reinforcement learning framework that addresses entropy collapse in GRPO by providing targeted exploration mechanisms and stabilization controls, improving reasoning performance in LLMs and VLMs.
AI.motivation: GRPO suffers from entropy collapse causing premature convergence and loss of diversity, while existing exploration methods introduce bias/variance that destabilizes optimization. There's a need for better exploration-stabilization balance in RL for reasoning tasks.
AI.method: UEC-RL introduces two components: 1) Targeted exploration that activates more exploration on difficult prompts to search for valuable reasoning trajectories, and 2) A stabilizer that prevents entropy from growing uncontrollably to maintain training stability while consolidating reliable behaviors.
AI.result: Experiments on LLM and VLM reasoning tasks show consistent gains over RL baselines on Pass@1 and Pass@k metrics. On Geometry3K, UEC-RL achieves 37.9% relative improvement over GRPO, demonstrating effective exploration without compromising convergence.
AI.conclusion: UEC-RL successfully addresses entropy collapse in RL-based reasoning, providing a key framework for scaling RL in large models by balancing exploration and stabilization throughout training.

title: Chain-of-Glimpse: Search-Guided Progressive Object-Grounded Reasoning for Video Understanding
summary: Video understanding requires identifying and reasoning over semantically discriminative visual objects across frames, yet existing object-agnostic solutions struggle to effectively handle substantial object variations over time. To address this, we introduce Chain-of-Glimpse, a search-guided progressive object-grounded reasoning framework that explicitly anchors each reasoning step to specific visual evidence regions, enabling compositional and multi-step decision-making. Formally, Chain-of-Glimpse formulates video reasoning as a step-by-step process that incrementally builds spatially grounded traces around task-relevant visual objects, thereby mitigating over-reliance on saliency-driven cues. Specifically, Chain-of-Glimpse features a search-guided controller, optimized via reinforcement learning with a format reward that significantly incentivizes grounding capability, to iteratively ground visual evidence regions and form reliable reasoning trajectories, yielding accurate and interpretable multi-step decisions. Extensive evaluations on both in domain NExTQA and out-of-domain Video-Holmes, CG-Bench Reasoning, and VRBench benchmarks demonstrate consistent performance gains, robustness and generalization of Chain-of-Glimpse across diverse video reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: Chain-of-Glimpse is a search-guided progressive object-grounded reasoning framework for video understanding that anchors reasoning steps to specific visual evidence regions, enabling compositional multi-step decision-making.
AI.motivation: Existing object-agnostic video understanding solutions struggle with substantial object variations over time and over-rely on saliency-driven cues rather than explicit visual grounding.
AI.method: Formulates video reasoning as step-by-step process building spatially grounded traces around task-relevant objects. Uses search-guided controller optimized via reinforcement learning with format reward to incentivize grounding capability, iteratively grounding visual evidence regions to form reliable reasoning trajectories.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations on NExTQA, Video-Holmes, CG-Bench Reasoning, and VRBench benchmarks demonstrate consistent performance gains, robustness, and generalization across diverse video reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: Chain-of-Glimpse effectively addresses object variation challenges in video understanding through explicit object-grounded reasoning, providing accurate and interpretable multi-step decisions with strong generalization capabilities.

title: Generative Augmented Inference
summary: Data-driven operations management often relies on parameters estimated from costly human-generated labels. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) and other AI systems offer inexpensive auxiliary data, but introduce a new challenge: AI outputs are not direct observations of the target outcomes, but could involve high-dimensional representations with complex and unknown relationships to human labels. Conventional methods leverage AI predictions as direct proxies for true labels, which can be inefficient or unreliable when this relationship is weak or misspecified. We propose Generative Augmented Inference (GAI), a general framework that incorporates AI-generated outputs as informative features for estimating models of human-labeled outcomes. GAI uses an orthogonal moment construction that enables consistent estimation and valid inference with flexible, nonparametric relationship between LLM-generated outputs and human labels. We establish asymptotic normality and show a "safe default" property: relative to human-data-only estimators, GAI weakly improves estimation efficiency under arbitrary auxiliary signals and yields strict gains whenever the auxiliary information is predictive. Empirically, GAI outperforms benchmarks across diverse settings. In conjoint analysis with weak auxiliary signals, GAI reduces estimation error by about 50% and lowers human labeling requirements by over 75%. In retail pricing, where all methods access the same auxiliary inputs, GAI consistently outperforms alternative estimators, highlighting the value of its construction rather than differences in information. In health insurance choice, it cuts labeling requirements by over 90% while maintaining decision accuracy. Across applications, GAI improves confidence interval coverage without inflating width. Overall, GAI provides a principled and scalable approach to integrating AI-generated information.

AI.tldr: GAI framework uses AI-generated outputs as features for estimating human-labeled outcomes, improving efficiency and reducing labeling requirements through orthogonal moment construction.
AI.motivation: AI-generated data is cheap but has complex, unknown relationships to human labels. Using AI predictions as direct proxies can be inefficient or unreliable when relationships are weak or misspecified.
AI.method: Generative Augmented Inference (GAI) incorporates AI-generated outputs as informative features using orthogonal moment construction, enabling consistent estimation with flexible, nonparametric relationships between AI outputs and human labels.
AI.result: GAI weakly improves estimation efficiency under arbitrary auxiliary signals and yields strict gains when auxiliary information is predictive. Empirically reduces estimation error by 50%, labeling requirements by 75-90%, and improves confidence interval coverage.
AI.conclusion: GAI provides a principled, scalable approach to integrating AI-generated information that outperforms benchmarks across diverse applications while maintaining statistical validity.

title: AgentGA: Evolving Code Solutions in Agent-Seed Space
summary: We present AgentGA, a framework that evolves autonomous code-generation runs by optimizing the agent seed: the task prompt plus optional parent archives that initialize a fresh workspace. The outer loop searches over these reusable starting conditions rather than editing code directly. Each generation launches a fresh autonomous run from a reset workspace, while selected parent archives provide inherited artifacts that descendants can inspect and reuse. AgentGA couples a population-level genetic algorithm with long-horizon agents; selection uses deterministic 1:1 elite tournaments and operator allocation is adapted online with a modified Hedge controller. We instantiate the approach for tabular AutoML on the 16-competition Weco-Kaggle Lite benchmark. On the 10 benchmark runs reported here, AgentGA averages 74.52% Exceeds % of Human versus 54.15% for AIDE. Across 1135 parent-child comparisons, descendants given parent archives outperform runs started from scratch, indicating that inherited artifacts improve later autonomous runs. These findings support agent-seed optimization as a practical design point for autonomous code-search systems.

AI.tldr: AgentGA evolves autonomous code-generation by optimizing agent seeds (task prompts + parent archives) rather than editing code directly, using genetic algorithms with long-horizon agents for tabular AutoML.
AI.motivation: To improve autonomous code-search systems by optimizing reusable starting conditions (agent seeds) rather than directly editing code, enabling better performance through inherited artifacts from previous runs.
AI.method: Framework with outer loop genetic algorithm searching over agent seeds (task prompts + optional parent archives). Each generation launches fresh autonomous runs from reset workspaces, with parent archives providing inheritable artifacts. Uses deterministic 1:1 elite tournaments and modified Hedge controller for online operator allocation adaptation.
AI.result: On Weco-Kaggle Lite benchmark (10 runs), AgentGA averages 74.52% Exceeds % of Human vs 54.15% for AIDE. Across 1135 parent-child comparisons, descendants with parent archives outperform runs started from scratch, showing inherited artifacts improve autonomous runs.
AI.conclusion: Agent-seed optimization is a practical design point for autonomous code-search systems, demonstrating that inherited artifacts from parent archives significantly improve performance of later autonomous runs.

title: The Courtroom Trial of Pixels: Robust Image Manipulation Localization via Adversarial Evidence and Reinforcement Learning Judgment
summary: Although some existing image manipulation localization (IML) methods incorporate authenticity-related supervision, this information is typically utilized merely as an auxiliary training signal to enhance the model's sensitivity to manipulation artifacts, rather than being explicitly modeled as localization evidence opposing the manipulated regions. Consequently, when manipulation traces are subtle or degraded by post-processing and noise, these methods struggle to explicitly compare manipulated and authentic evidence, resulting in unreliable predictions in ambiguous areas. To address these issues, we propose a courtroom-style adjudication framework that regards IML task as the confrontation of evidence followed by judgment. The framework comprises a prosecution stream, a defense stream, and a judge model. We first build a dual-hypothesis segmentation architecture on a shared multi-scale encoder, in which the prosecution stream asserts manipulation and the defense stream asserts authenticity. Guided by edge priors, it produces evidence for manipulated and authentic regions through cascaded multi-level fusion, bidirectional disagreement suppression, and dynamic debate refinement. We further develop a reinforcement learning judge model that performs strategic re-inference and refinement on uncertain regions, yielding a manipulated-region mask. The judge model is trained with advantage-based rewards and a soft-IoU objective, and reliability is calibrated via entropy and cross-hypothesis consistency. Experimental results show that our model achieves superior average performance compared with SOTA IML methods.

AI.tldr: A courtroom-style adjudication framework for image manipulation localization that treats the task as evidence confrontation followed by judgment, achieving superior performance over state-of-the-art methods.
AI.motivation: Existing IML methods use authenticity supervision only as auxiliary training signals rather than explicitly modeling it as localization evidence. They struggle with subtle manipulations or degraded traces, failing to explicitly compare manipulated vs. authentic evidence in ambiguous areas.
AI.method: Proposes a courtroom-style framework with prosecution stream (asserts manipulation), defense stream (asserts authenticity), and judge model. Uses dual-hypothesis segmentation on shared encoder with edge priors, cascaded multi-level fusion, bidirectional disagreement suppression, and dynamic debate refinement. Judge model uses reinforcement learning with strategic re-inference on uncertain regions, trained with advantage-based rewards and soft-IoU objective.
AI.result: The model achieves superior average performance compared with state-of-the-art IML methods in experimental evaluations.
AI.conclusion: The courtroom-style adjudication framework effectively addresses limitations of existing IML methods by explicitly modeling and comparing manipulated vs. authentic evidence, leading to more reliable predictions especially in challenging cases with subtle manipulations.

title: From Risk to Rescue: An Agentic Survival Analysis Framework for Liquidation Prevention
summary: Decentralized Finance (DeFi) lending protocols like Aave v3 rely on over-collateralization to secure loans, yet users frequently face liquidation due to volatile market conditions. Existing risk management tools utilize static health-factor thresholds, which are reactive and fail to distinguish between administrative "dust" cleanup and genuine insolvency. In this work, we propose an autonomous agent that leverages time-to-event (survival) analysis and moves beyond prediction to execution. Unlike passive risk signals, this agent perceives risk, simulates counterfactual futures, and executes protocol-faithful interventions to proactively prevent liquidations. We introduce a return period metric derived from a numerically stable XGBoost Cox proportional hazards model to normalize risk across transaction types, coupled with a volatility-adjusted trend score to filter transient market noise. To select optimal interventions, we implement a counterfactual optimization loop that simulates potential user actions to find the minimum capital required to mitigate risk. We validate our approach using a high-fidelity, protocol-faithful Aave v3 simulator on a cohort of 4,882 high-risk user profiles. The results demonstrate the agent's ability to prevent liquidations in imminent-risk scenarios where static rules fail, effectively "saving the unsavable" while maintaining a zero worsening rate, providing a critical safety guarantee often missing in autonomous financial agents. Furthermore, the system successfully differentiates between actionable financial risks and negligible dust events, optimizing capital efficiency where static rules fail.

AI.tldr: An autonomous agent using survival analysis and counterfactual optimization to proactively prevent liquidations in DeFi lending protocols, distinguishing between real insolvency and dust cleanup.
AI.motivation: Current DeFi lending protocols use reactive static health-factor thresholds that fail to distinguish between genuine insolvency and administrative dust cleanup, leading to unnecessary liquidations during market volatility.
AI.method: Uses time-to-event (survival) analysis with XGBoost Cox proportional hazards model for risk normalization, volatility-adjusted trend scoring to filter market noise, and counterfactual optimization loop to simulate user actions and find minimum capital interventions.
AI.result: Validated on 4,882 high-risk Aave v3 user profiles, the agent prevented liquidations in imminent-risk scenarios where static rules failed, maintained zero worsening rate, and successfully differentiated between actionable risks and dust events.
AI.conclusion: The autonomous agent provides proactive risk management with critical safety guarantees, optimizing capital efficiency while preventing unnecessary liquidations in DeFi lending protocols.

title: Rethinking Patient Education as Multi-turn Multi-modal Interaction
comment: Equal contribution for the first two authors
summary: Most medical multimodal benchmarks focus on static tasks such as image question answering, report generation, and plain-language rewriting. Patient education is more demanding: systems must identify relevant evidence across images, show patients where to look, explain findings in accessible language, and handle confusion or distress. Yet most patient education work remains text-only, even though combined image-and-text explanations may better support understanding. We introduce MedImageEdu, a benchmark for multi-turn, evidence-grounded radiology patient education. Each case provides a radiology report with report text and case images. A DoctorAgent interacts with a PatientAgent, conditioned on a hidden profile that captures factors such as education level, health literacy, and personality. When a patient question would benefit from visual support, the DoctorAgent can issue drawing instructions grounded in the report, case images, and the current question to a benchmark-provided drawing tool. The tool returns image(s), after which the DoctorAgent produces a final multimodal response consisting of the image(s) and a grounded plain-language explanation. MedImageEdu contains 150 cases from three sources and evaluates both the consultation process and the final multimodal response along five dimensions: Consultation, Safety and Scope, Language Quality, Drawing Quality, and Image-Text Response Quality. Across representative open- and closed-source vision-language model agents, we find three consistent gaps: fluent language often outpaces faithful visual grounding, safety is the weakest dimension across disease categories, and emotionally tense interactions are harder than low education or low health literacy. MedImageEdu provides a controlled testbed for assessing whether multimodal agents can teach from evidence rather than merely answer from text.

AI.tldr: MedImageEdu is a benchmark for multi-turn, evidence-grounded radiology patient education that evaluates multimodal agents' ability to provide visual explanations alongside plain-language responses during doctor-patient consultations.
AI.motivation: Current medical multimodal benchmarks focus on static tasks like image QA and report generation, but patient education requires more demanding capabilities: identifying relevant evidence across images, showing patients where to look, explaining findings in accessible language, and handling emotional responses. Most patient education work remains text-only despite evidence that combined image-and-text explanations better support understanding.
AI.method: MedImageEdu uses a multi-turn consultation framework where a DoctorAgent interacts with a PatientAgent conditioned on hidden patient profiles (education level, health literacy, personality). When visual support is needed, the DoctorAgent issues drawing instructions grounded in radiology reports, case images, and current questions to a benchmark-provided drawing tool, then produces final multimodal responses combining images with grounded plain-language explanations.
AI.result: The benchmark contains 150 cases from three sources and evaluates both consultation process and final multimodal responses across five dimensions. Testing representative vision-language model agents revealed three consistent gaps: fluent language often outpaces faithful visual grounding, safety is the weakest dimension across disease categories, and emotionally tense interactions are harder than low education or low health literacy.
AI.conclusion: MedImageEdu provides a controlled testbed for assessing whether multimodal agents can teach from evidence rather than merely answer from text, addressing critical gaps in current medical AI evaluation and advancing patient education capabilities.

title: NG-GS: NeRF-Guided 3D Gaussian Splatting Segmentation
summary: Recent advances in 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) have enabled highly efficient and photorealistic novel view synthesis. However, segmenting objects accurately in 3DGS remains challenging due to the discrete nature of Gaussian representations, which often leads to aliasing and artifacts at object boundaries. In this paper, we introduce NG-GS, a novel framework for high-quality object segmentation in 3DGS that explicitly addresses boundary discretization. Our approach begins by automatically identifying ambiguous Gaussians at object boundaries using mask variance analysis. We then apply radial basis function (RBF) interpolation to construct a spatially continuous feature field, enhanced by multi-resolution hash encoding for efficient multi-scale representation. A joint optimization strategy aligns 3DGS with a lightweight NeRF module through alignment and spatial continuity losses, ensuring smooth and consistent segmentation boundaries. Extensive experiments on NVOS, LERF-OVS, and ScanNet benchmarks demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance, with significant gains in boundary mIoU. Code is available at https://github.com/BJTU-KD3D/NG-GS.

AI.tldr: NG-GS is a novel framework for high-quality object segmentation in 3D Gaussian Splatting that addresses boundary discretization issues through RBF interpolation and joint optimization with NeRF.
AI.motivation: Segmenting objects in 3D Gaussian Splatting is challenging due to the discrete nature of Gaussian representations, which causes aliasing and artifacts at object boundaries.
AI.method: 1) Automatically identify ambiguous Gaussians at boundaries using mask variance analysis; 2) Apply RBF interpolation to create continuous feature field with multi-resolution hash encoding; 3) Joint optimization aligning 3DGS with lightweight NeRF module using alignment and spatial continuity losses.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on NVOS, LERF-OVS, and ScanNet benchmarks with significant gains in boundary mIoU.
AI.conclusion: NG-GS effectively addresses boundary discretization in 3DGS segmentation through continuous feature representation and joint optimization, enabling high-quality object segmentation.

title: CLion: Efficient Cautious Lion Optimizer with Enhanced Generalization
summary:
Lion optimizer is a popular learning-based optimization algorithm in machine learning, which shows impressive performance in training many deep learning models. Although convergence property of the Lion optimizer has been studied, its generalization analysis is still missing. To fill this gap, we study generalization property of the Lion via algorithmic stability based on the mathematical induction. Specifically, we prove that the Lion has a generalization error of $O(\frac{1}{Nτ^T})$, where $N$ is training sample size, and $τ>0$ denotes the smallest absolute value of non-zero element in gradient estimator, and $T$ is the total iteration number. In addition, we obtain an interesting byproduct that the SignSGD algorithm has the same generalization error as the Lion. To enhance generalization of the Lion, we design a novel efficient Cautious Lion (i.e., CLion) optimizer by cautiously using sign function. Moreover, we prove that our CLion has a lower generalization error of $O(\frac{1}{N})$ than $O(\frac{1}{Nτ^T})$ of the Lion, since the parameter $τ$ generally is very small. Meanwhile, we study convergence property of our CLion optimizer, and prove that our CLion has a fast convergence rate of $O(\frac{\sqrt{d}}{T^{1/4}})$ under $\ell_1$-norm of gradient for nonconvex stochastic optimization, where $d$ denotes the model dimension.
  Extensive numerical experiments demonstrate effectiveness of our CLion optimizer.

AI.tldr: This paper provides the first generalization analysis of the Lion optimizer, proves it has O(1/(Nτ^T)) generalization error, shows SignSGD has the same error, proposes a new CLion optimizer with improved O(1/N) generalization error, and proves CLion has O(√d/T^{1/4}) convergence rate.
AI.motivation: While the Lion optimizer has shown impressive performance in training deep learning models and its convergence properties have been studied, there is a significant gap in understanding its generalization capabilities. The authors aim to provide the first generalization analysis of Lion and develop an improved optimizer with better generalization properties.
AI.method: The authors use algorithmic stability based on mathematical induction to analyze Lion's generalization error. They then design a novel Cautious Lion (CLion) optimizer that cautiously uses the sign function to enhance generalization. They prove generalization bounds for both Lion and CLion, and analyze convergence properties of CLion for nonconvex stochastic optimization.
AI.result: The paper proves Lion has generalization error O(1/(Nτ^T)) where τ is the smallest absolute value of non-zero gradient elements. SignSGD has the same error. CLion achieves improved generalization error O(1/N) and convergence rate O(√d/T^{1/4}) under ℓ₁-norm gradient for nonconvex optimization. Extensive experiments validate CLion's effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: This work fills the generalization analysis gap for Lion optimizer, provides theoretical generalization bounds, reveals SignSGD shares Lion's generalization properties, and proposes CLion as an improved optimizer with both better generalization error and convergence guarantees, validated by experiments.

title: Acceptance Dynamics Across Cognitive Domains in Speculative Decoding
summary: Speculative decoding accelerates large language model (LLM) inference. It uses a small draft model to propose a tree of future tokens. A larger target model then verifies these tokens in a single batched forward pass. Despite the growing body of work on speculative methods, the degree to which the cognitive characteristics of a task affect acceptance probability remains largely unexplored. We present an empirical study of tree-based speculative decoding acceptance dynamics. Our study spans four well-established NLP benchmark domains: code generation, mathematical reasoning, logical reasoning, and open-ended chat. For this, we use TinyLlama-1.1B as the draft model against Llama-2-7B-Chat-GPTQ as the target. Over 99,768 speculative nodes collected from 200 prompts, we derive per-domain acceptance rates, expected accepted lengths, depth-acceptance profiles, and entropy-acceptance correlations. We find that task type is a stronger predictor of acceptance than tree depth. Furthermore, only the chat domain consistently yields an expected accepted length exceeding 1.0 token per step. We also show that the entropy-acceptance correlation is consistently negative but weak across all domains (rho in [-0.20, -0.15]). Counterintuitively, chat produces the highest entropy yet the highest acceptance rate. We attribute this divergence to the lexical predictability of RLHF-aligned register. These findings have direct implications for domain-aware speculation budgets and draft-model selection strategies. Index Terms--speculative decoding, large language model inference, tree attention, draft model, acceptance probability, LLM efficiency

AI.tldr: Empirical study shows task type (code, math, logic, chat) affects speculative decoding acceptance more than tree depth, with chat having highest acceptance despite highest entropy.
AI.motivation: Despite growing work on speculative decoding, little is known about how task cognitive characteristics affect acceptance probability. The study aims to empirically investigate acceptance dynamics across different NLP domains.
AI.method: Used TinyLlama-1.1B as draft model and Llama-2-7B-Chat-GPTQ as target. Collected 99,768 speculative nodes from 200 prompts across four domains: code generation, mathematical reasoning, logical reasoning, and open-ended chat. Analyzed per-domain acceptance rates, expected accepted lengths, depth-acceptance profiles, and entropy-acceptance correlations.
AI.result: Task type is stronger predictor of acceptance than tree depth. Only chat domain consistently yields expected accepted length >1.0 token per step. Entropy-acceptance correlation is consistently negative but weak (rho in [-0.20, -0.15]). Counterintuitively, chat produces highest entropy yet highest acceptance rate.
AI.conclusion: Task cognitive characteristics significantly impact speculative decoding acceptance. Chat's high acceptance despite high entropy attributed to lexical predictability of RLHF-aligned register. Findings have implications for domain-aware speculation budgets and draft-model selection strategies.

title: G-MIXER: Geodesic Mixup-based Implicit Semantic Expansion and Explicit Semantic Re-ranking for Zero-Shot Composed Image Retrieval
summary: Composed Image Retrieval (CIR) aims to retrieve target images by integrating a reference image with a corresponding modification text. CIR requires jointly considering the explicit semantics specified in the query and the implicit semantics embedded within its bi-modal composition. Recent training-free Zero-Shot CIR (ZS-CIR) methods leverage Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) to generate detailed target descriptions, converting the implicit information into explicit textual expressions. However, these methods rely heavily on the textual modality and fail to capture the fuzzy retrieval nature that requires considering diverse combinations of candidates. This leads to reduced diversity and accuracy in retrieval results. To address this limitation, we propose a novel training-free method, Geodesic Mixup-based Implicit semantic eXpansion and Explicit semantic Re-ranking for ZS-CIR (G-MIXER). G-MIXER constructs composed query features that reflect the implicit semantics of reference image-text pairs through geodesic mixup over a range of mixup ratios, and builds a diverse candidate set. The generated candidates are then re-ranked using explicit semantics derived from MLLMs, improving both retrieval diversity and accuracy. Our proposed G-MIXER achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple ZS-CIR benchmarks, effectively handling both implicit and explicit semantics without additional training. Our code will be available at https://github.com/maya0395/gmixer.

AI.tldr: G-MIXER is a training-free ZS-CIR method that uses geodesic mixup to capture implicit semantics and MLLM-based re-ranking for explicit semantics, achieving SOTA performance without additional training.
AI.motivation: Existing ZS-CIR methods rely too heavily on textual modality and fail to capture the fuzzy retrieval nature that requires considering diverse candidate combinations, leading to reduced diversity and accuracy in retrieval results.
AI.method: G-MIXER constructs composed query features through geodesic mixup over various ratios to capture implicit semantics, builds diverse candidate sets, then re-ranks candidates using explicit semantics derived from MLLMs.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple ZS-CIR benchmarks, effectively handling both implicit and explicit semantics without additional training.
AI.conclusion: G-MIXER successfully addresses limitations of previous ZS-CIR methods by combining geodesic mixup for implicit semantics with MLLM-based re-ranking for explicit semantics, improving both diversity and accuracy in composed image retrieval.

title: ConfLayers: Adaptive Confidence-based Layer Skipping for Self-Speculative Decoding
summary: Self-speculative decoding is an inference technique for large language models designed to speed up generation without sacrificing output quality. It combines fast, approximate decoding using a compact version of the model as a draft model with selective re-evaluation by the full target model. Some existing methods form the draft model by dynamically learning which layers to skip during inference, effectively creating a smaller subnetwork to speed up computation. However, using heuristic-based approaches to select layers to skip can often be simpler and more effective. In this paper, we propose ConfLayers, a dynamic plug-and-play approach to forming the draft model in self-speculative decoding via confidence-based intermediate layer skipping. The process iteratively computes confidence scores for all layers, selects layers to skip based on an adaptive threshold, evaluates the performance of the resulting set, and updates the best selection until no further improvement is achieved or a maximum number of iterations is reached. This framework avoids the overhead and complexity of training a layer skipping policy and can provide more consistent speed-quality trade-offs while preserving the adaptivity of the draft model to diverse tasks and datasets. The performance evaluation of ConfLayers across different models and datasets shows that our novel approach offers up to 1.4x speedup over vanilla LLM generation.

AI.tldr: ConfLayers is a dynamic plug-and-play approach for self-speculative decoding that uses confidence-based intermediate layer skipping to speed up LLM inference without training overhead.
AI.motivation: Existing self-speculative decoding methods that use learned layer skipping policies can be complex and may not always be optimal. Heuristic-based approaches are simpler but lack adaptivity. There's a need for a method that combines simplicity with effective dynamic layer selection.
AI.method: ConfLayers uses an iterative confidence-based approach: computes confidence scores for all layers, selects layers to skip based on adaptive thresholds, evaluates performance, and updates the best selection until convergence or max iterations. This creates a draft model without training overhead.
AI.result: ConfLayers achieves up to 1.4x speedup over vanilla LLM generation while maintaining output quality. It provides consistent speed-quality trade-offs and preserves adaptivity to diverse tasks and datasets.
AI.conclusion: ConfLayers offers an effective, training-free approach to self-speculative decoding that balances simplicity with performance, providing significant speedup while maintaining the adaptivity needed for diverse applications.

title: DR$^{3}$-Eval: Towards Realistic and Reproducible Deep Research Evaluation
summary: Deep Research Agents (DRAs) aim to solve complex, long-horizon research tasks involving planning, retrieval, multimodal understanding, and report generation, yet their evaluation remains challenging due to dynamic web environments and ambiguous task definitions. We propose DR$^{3}$-Eval, a realistic and reproducible benchmark for evaluating deep research agents on multimodal, multi-file report generation. DR$^{3}$-Eval is constructed from authentic user-provided materials and paired with a per-task static research sandbox corpus that simulates open-web complexity while remaining fully verifiable, containing supportive documents, distractors, and noise. Moreover, we introduce a multi-dimensional evaluation framework measuring Information Recall, Factual Accuracy, Citation Coverage, Instruction Following, and Depth Quality, and validate its alignment with human judgments. Experiments with our developed multi-agent system DR$^{3}$-Agent based on multiple state-of-the-art language models demonstrate that DR$^{3}$-Eval is highly challenging and reveals critical failure modes in retrieval robustness and hallucination control. Our code and data are publicly available.

AI.tldr: DR³-Eval is a realistic benchmark for evaluating deep research agents on multimodal, multi-file report generation, featuring authentic materials, static research sandboxes, and a multi-dimensional evaluation framework validated against human judgments.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation of Deep Research Agents (DRAs) is challenging due to dynamic web environments and ambiguous task definitions, creating a need for realistic, reproducible benchmarks.
AI.method: DR³-Eval constructs benchmarks from authentic user-provided materials with per-task static research sandbox corpora containing supportive documents, distractors, and noise. It introduces a multi-dimensional evaluation framework measuring Information Recall, Factual Accuracy, Citation Coverage, Instruction Following, and Depth Quality.
AI.result: Experiments with DR³-Agent (multi-agent system based on state-of-the-art language models) show DR³-Eval is highly challenging and reveals critical failure modes in retrieval robustness and hallucination control.
AI.conclusion: DR³-Eval provides a realistic, reproducible benchmark for evaluating deep research agents, with validated evaluation metrics and publicly available code/data, addressing current evaluation challenges in the field.

title: MS-SSE-Net: A Multi-Scale Spatial Squeeze-and-Excitation Network for Structural Damage Detection in Civil and Geotechnical Engineering
summary: Structural damage detection is essential for maintaining the safety and reliability of civil infrastructure. However, accurately identifying different types of structural damage from images remains challenging due to variations in damage patterns and environmental conditions. To address these challenges, this paper proposes MS-SSE-Net, a novel deep learning (DL) framework for structural damage classification. The proposed model is built upon the DenseNet201 backbone and integrates novel multi-scale feature extraction with channel and spatial attention mechanisms (MS-SSE-Net). Specifically, parallel depthwise convolutions capture both local and contextual features, while squeeze-and-excitation style channel attention and spatial attention emphasize informative regions and suppress irrelevant noise. The refined features are then processed through global average pooling and a fully connected classification layer to generate the final predictions. Experiments are conducted on the StructDamage dataset containing multiple structural damage categories. The proposed MS-SSE-Net demonstrates superior performance compared with the baseline DenseNet201 and other comparative approaches. Specifically, the proposed method achieves 99.31% precision, 99.25% recall, 99.27% F1-score, and 99.26% accuracy, outperforming the baseline model which achieved 98.62% precision, 98.53% recall, 98.58% F1-score, and 98.53% accuracy.

AI.tldr: MS-SSE-Net: A novel deep learning framework using DenseNet201 backbone with multi-scale feature extraction and attention mechanisms for structural damage classification from images, achieving state-of-the-art performance on the StructDamage dataset.
AI.motivation: Structural damage detection is crucial for infrastructure safety, but accurately identifying different damage types from images is challenging due to variations in damage patterns and environmental conditions.
AI.method: Built on DenseNet201 backbone, integrates multi-scale feature extraction with channel and spatial attention mechanisms. Uses parallel depthwise convolutions to capture local and contextual features, with squeeze-and-excitation style channel attention and spatial attention to emphasize informative regions and suppress noise.
AI.result: Achieves 99.31% precision, 99.25% recall, 99.27% F1-score, and 99.26% accuracy on StructDamage dataset, outperforming baseline DenseNet201 (98.62% precision, 98.53% recall, 98.58% F1-score, 98.53% accuracy) and other comparative approaches.
AI.conclusion: MS-SSE-Net demonstrates superior performance for structural damage classification, effectively addressing challenges of damage pattern variations and environmental conditions through its multi-scale attention mechanisms.

title: ELMoE-3D: Leveraging Intrinsic Elasticity of MoE for Hybrid-Bonding-Enabled Self-Speculative Decoding in On-Premises Serving
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models have become the dominant architecture for large-scale language models, yet on-premises serving remains fundamentally memory-bound as batching turns sparse per-token compute into dense memory activation. Memory-centric architectures (PIM, NMP) improve bandwidth but leave compute underutilized under MoE's low arithmetic intensity at high batch sizes. Speculative decoding (SD) trades idle compute for fewer target invocations, yet verification must load experts even for rejected tokens, severely limiting its benefit in MoE especially at low batch sizes. We propose ELMoE-3D, a hybrid-bonding (HB)-based HW-SW co-designed framework that unifies cache-based acceleration and speculative decoding to offer overall speedup across batch sizes. We identify two intrinsic elasticity axes of MoE-expert and bit-and jointly scale them to construct Elastic Self-Speculative Decoding (Elastic-SD), which serves as both an expert cache and a strongly aligned self-draft model accelerated by high HB bandwidth. Our LSB-augmented bit-sliced architecture exploits inherent redundancy in bit-slice representations to natively support bit-nested execution. On our 3D-stacked hardware, ELMoE-3D achieves an average $6.6\times$ speedup and $4.4\times$ energy efficiency gain over naive MoE serving on xPU across batch sizes 1-16, and delivers $2.2\times$ speedup and $1.4\times$ energy efficiency gain over the best-performing prior accelerator baseline.

AI.tldr: ELMoE-3D is a hardware-software co-designed framework that combines cache-based acceleration with speculative decoding for Mixture-of-Experts models, achieving significant speedup and energy efficiency improvements across various batch sizes.
AI.motivation: Current MoE serving faces memory bottlenecks, with memory-centric architectures underutilizing compute and speculative decoding suffering from expert loading overhead even for rejected tokens, especially at low batch sizes.
AI.method: Proposes ELMoE-3D framework with Elastic Self-Speculative Decoding that scales expert and bit dimensions, uses LSB-augmented bit-sliced architecture for bit-nested execution, and leverages 3D-stacked hardware with hybrid bonding for high bandwidth.
AI.result: Achieves average 6.6× speedup and 4.4× energy efficiency gain over naive MoE serving across batch sizes 1-16, and 2.2× speedup and 1.4× energy efficiency gain over best prior accelerator baseline.
AI.conclusion: ELMoE-3D successfully addresses MoE serving bottlenecks by unifying cache acceleration and speculative decoding through hardware-software co-design, demonstrating significant performance improvements across different batch sizes.

title: M2-PALE: A Framework for Explaining Multi-Agent MCTS--Minimax Hybrids via Process Mining and LLMs
summary: Monte-Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) is a fundamental sampling-based search algorithm widely used for online planning in sequential decision-making domains. Despite its success in driving recent advances in artificial intelligence, understanding the behavior of MCTS agents remains a challenge for both developers and users. This difficulty stems from the complex search trees produced through the simulation of numerous future states and their intricate relationships. A known weakness of standard MCTS is its reliance on highly selective tree construction, which may lead to the omission of crucial moves and a vulnerability to tactical traps. To resolve this, we incorporate shallow, full-width Minimax search into the rollout phase of multi-agent MCTS to enhance strategic depth. Furthermore, to demystify the resulting decision-making logic, we introduce \textsf{M2-PALE} (MCTS--Minimax Process-Aided Linguistic Explanations). This framework employs process mining techniques, specifically the Alpha Miner, iDHM, and Inductive Miner algorithms, to extract underlying behavioral workflows from agent execution traces. These process models are then synthesized by LLMs to generate human-readable causal and distal explanations. We demonstrate the efficacy of our approach in a small-scale checkers environment, establishing a scalable foundation for interpreting hybrid agents in increasingly complex strategic domains.

AI.tldr: MCTS-Minimax hybrid agent with process mining and LLM explanations for interpretable strategic decision-making in checkers.
AI.motivation: MCTS agents are hard to understand due to complex search trees, and standard MCTS has weaknesses in selective tree construction that can miss crucial moves and be vulnerable to tactical traps. Need for more interpretable strategic agents.
AI.method: 1) Incorporate shallow, full-width Minimax search into MCTS rollout phase for enhanced strategic depth. 2) M2-PALE framework: Use process mining (Alpha Miner, iDHM, Inductive Miner) to extract behavioral workflows from agent execution traces, then synthesize process models with LLMs to generate human-readable causal and distal explanations.
AI.result: Demonstrated efficacy in small-scale checkers environment, establishing scalable foundation for interpreting hybrid agents in complex strategic domains.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid MCTS-Minimax approach with process mining and LLM explanations provides both improved strategic performance and interpretability, addressing key weaknesses of standard MCTS while making agent decision-making more transparent.

title: Data Synthesis Improves 3D Myotube Instance Segmentation
comment: 4 pages, 4 figures, submitted to BMT (VDE) 2026 Conference
summary: Myotubes are multinucleated muscle fibers serving as key model systems for studying muscle physiology, disease mechanisms, and drug responses. Mechanistic studies and drug screening thereby rely on quantitative morphological readouts such as diameter, length, and branching degree, which in turn require precise three-dimensional instance segmentation. Yet established pretrained biomedical segmentation models fail to generalize to this domain due to the absence of large annotated myotube datasets. We introduce a geometry-driven synthesis pipeline that models individual myotubes via polynomial centerlines, locally varying radii, branching structures, and ellipsoidal end caps derived from real microscopy observations. Synthetic volumes are rendered with realistic noise, optical artifacts, and CycleGAN-based Domain Adaptation (DA). A compact 3D U-Net with self-supervised encoder pretraining, trained exclusively on synthetic data, achieves a mean IPQ of 0.22 on real data, significantly outperforming three established zero-shot segmentation models, demonstrating that biophysics-driven synthesis enables effective instance segmentation in annotation-scarce biomedical domains.

AI.tldr: A geometry-driven synthesis pipeline creates realistic synthetic myotube data for training 3D instance segmentation models, achieving state-of-the-art performance without real annotations.
AI.motivation: Myotube research requires precise 3D instance segmentation for morphological analysis, but existing biomedical segmentation models fail due to lack of large annotated datasets in this domain.
AI.method: Geometry-driven synthesis pipeline models individual myotubes using polynomial centerlines, varying radii, branching structures, and ellipsoidal end caps. Synthetic volumes include realistic noise, optical artifacts, and CycleGAN-based Domain Adaptation. A compact 3D U-Net with self-supervised encoder pretraining is trained exclusively on synthetic data.
AI.result: The model achieves mean IPQ of 0.22 on real data, significantly outperforming three established zero-shot segmentation models.
AI.conclusion: Biophysics-driven synthesis enables effective instance segmentation in annotation-scarce biomedical domains, providing a solution for myotube morphological analysis without requiring extensive real annotations.

title: Zeroth-Order Optimization at the Edge of Stability
summary: Zeroth-order (ZO) methods are widely used when gradients are unavailable or prohibitively expensive, including black-box learning and memory-efficient fine-tuning of large models, yet their optimization dynamics in deep learning remain underexplored. In this work, we provide an explicit step size condition that exactly captures the (mean-square) linear stability of a family of ZO methods based on the standard two-point estimator. Our characterization reveals a sharp contrast with first-order (FO) methods: whereas FO stability is governed solely by the largest Hessian eigenvalue, mean-square stability of ZO methods depends on the entire Hessian spectrum. Since computing the full Hessian spectrum is infeasible in practical neural network training, we further derive tractable stability bounds that depend only on the largest eigenvalue and the Hessian trace. Empirically, we find that full-batch ZO methods operate at the edge of stability: ZO-GD, ZO-GDM, and ZO-Adam consistently stabilize near the predicted stability boundary across a range of deep learning training problems. Our results highlight an implicit regularization effect specific to ZO methods, where large step sizes primarily regularize the Hessian trace, whereas in FO methods they regularize the top eigenvalue.

AI.tldr: ZO methods have different stability dynamics than FO methods - ZO stability depends on entire Hessian spectrum, not just largest eigenvalue, and ZO methods operate at edge of stability with implicit regularization on Hessian trace.
AI.motivation: ZO methods are important for black-box learning and memory-efficient fine-tuning of large models, but their optimization dynamics in deep learning remain poorly understood compared to FO methods.
AI.method: Provide explicit step size condition capturing mean-square linear stability of ZO methods based on standard two-point estimator. Derive tractable stability bounds using largest Hessian eigenvalue and Hessian trace since full spectrum computation is infeasible.
AI.result: ZO methods (ZO-GD, ZO-GDM, ZO-Adam) consistently stabilize near predicted stability boundary across deep learning training problems, operating at edge of stability. ZO stability depends on entire Hessian spectrum unlike FO methods.
AI.conclusion: ZO methods exhibit implicit regularization effect where large step sizes primarily regularize Hessian trace, whereas in FO methods they regularize top eigenvalue, revealing fundamental difference in optimization dynamics.

title: CAMO: An Agentic Framework for Automated Causal Discovery from Micro Behaviors to Macro Emergence in LLM Agent Simulations
summary: LLM-empowered agent simulations are increasingly used to study social emergence, yet the micro-to-macro causal mechanisms behind macro outcomes often remain unclear. This is challenging because emergence arises from intertwined agent interactions and meso-level feedback and nonlinearity, making generative mechanisms hard to disentangle. To this end, we introduce \textbf{\textsc{CAMO}}, an automated \textbf{Ca}usal discovery framework from \textbf{M}icr\textbf{o} behaviors to \textbf{M}acr\textbf{o} Emergence in LLM agent simulations. \textsc{CAMO} converts mechanistic hypotheses into computable factors grounded in simulation records and learns a compact causal representation centered on an emergent target $Y$. \textsc{CAMO} outputs a computable Markov boundary and a minimal upstream explanatory subgraph, yielding interpretable causal chains and actionable intervention levers. It also uses simulator-internal counterfactual probing to orient ambiguous edges and revise hypotheses when evidence contradicts the current view. Experiments across four emergent settings demonstrate the promise of \textsc{CAMO}.

AI.tldr: CAMO is an automated causal discovery framework that identifies micro-to-macro causal mechanisms in LLM agent simulations to explain emergent social phenomena.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-empowered agent simulations can study social emergence but lack clarity on the micro-to-macro causal mechanisms behind macro outcomes, making it hard to disentangle generative mechanisms due to intertwined interactions and nonlinear feedback.
AI.method: CAMO converts mechanistic hypotheses into computable factors from simulation records, learns compact causal representations centered on emergent targets, outputs computable Markov boundaries and minimal upstream explanatory subgraphs, and uses simulator-internal counterfactual probing to orient ambiguous edges and revise hypotheses.
AI.result: Experiments across four emergent settings demonstrate the promise of CAMO for identifying interpretable causal chains and actionable intervention levers in LLM agent simulations.
AI.conclusion: CAMO provides an automated framework for causal discovery from micro behaviors to macro emergence, addressing the challenge of understanding complex social emergence in LLM agent simulations through interpretable causal analysis.

title: Mean Flow Policy Optimization
summary: Diffusion models have recently emerged as expressive policy representations for online reinforcement learning (RL). However, their iterative generative processes introduce substantial training and inference overhead. To overcome this limitation, we propose to represent policies using MeanFlow models, a class of few-step flow-based generative models, to improve training and inference efficiency over diffusion-based RL approaches. To promote exploration, we optimize MeanFlow policies under the maximum entropy RL framework via soft policy iteration, and address two key challenges specific to MeanFlow policies: action likelihood evaluation and soft policy improvement. Experiments on MuJoCo and DeepMind Control Suite benchmarks demonstrate that our method, Mean Flow Policy Optimization (MFPO), achieves performance comparable to or exceeding current diffusion-based baselines while considerably reducing training and inference time. Our code is available at https://github.com/MFPolicy/MFPO.

AI.tldr: MFPO proposes MeanFlow models as efficient flow-based alternatives to diffusion policies for RL, achieving comparable performance with significantly reduced training/inference time.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models have shown promise as expressive policy representations in RL, but their iterative generative processes create substantial training and inference overhead that limits practical efficiency.
AI.method: Proposes MeanFlow models (few-step flow-based generative models) as policy representations, optimized under maximum entropy RL via soft policy iteration. Addresses two key challenges: action likelihood evaluation and soft policy improvement for MeanFlow policies.
AI.result: MFPO achieves performance comparable to or exceeding current diffusion-based baselines on MuJoCo and DeepMind Control Suite benchmarks while considerably reducing both training and inference time.
AI.conclusion: MeanFlow models provide an efficient alternative to diffusion policies for RL, maintaining expressive power while dramatically improving computational efficiency, making them more practical for real-world applications.

title: SynHAT: A Two-stage Coarse-to-Fine Diffusion Framework for Synthesizing Human Activity Traces
summary: Human activity traces (HATs) are critical for many applications, including human mobility modeling and point-of-interest (POI) recommendation. However, growing privacy concerns have severely limited access to authentic large-scale HAT datasets. Recent advances in generative AI provide new opportunities to synthesize realistic and privacy-preserving HATs for such applications. Yet two major challenges remain: (i) HATs are highly irregular and dynamic, with long and varying time intervals, making it difficult to capture their complex spatio-temporal dependencies and underlying distributions; and (ii) generative models are often computationally expensive, making long-term, fine-grained HAT synthesis inefficient. To address these challenges, we propose SynHAT, a computationally efficient coarse-to-fine HAT synthesis framework built on a novel spatio-temporal denoising diffusion model. In Stage 1, we develop Coarse-HADiff, which models the overall spatio-temporal dependencies of coarse-grained latent spatio-temporal traces. It incorporates a novel Latent Spatio-Temporal U-Net with dual Drift-Jitter branches to jointly model smooth spatial transitions and temporal variations during denoising. In Stage 2, we introduce a three-step pipeline consisting of Behavior Pattern Extraction, Fine-HADiff, which shares the same architecture as Coarse-HADiff, and Semantic Alignment to generate fine-grained latent spatio-temporal traces from the Stage 1 outputs. We extensively evaluate SynHAT in terms of data fidelity, utility, privacy, robustness, and scalability. Experiments on real-world HAT datasets from four cities across three countries show that SynHAT substantially outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving 52% and 33% improvements on spatial and temporal metrics, respectively.

AI.tldr: SynHAT is a coarse-to-fine diffusion framework for synthesizing privacy-preserving human activity traces that addresses irregular spatio-temporal patterns and computational efficiency challenges.
AI.motivation: Privacy concerns limit access to authentic large-scale human activity trace datasets needed for applications like mobility modeling and POI recommendation, while existing generative approaches struggle with irregular spatio-temporal patterns and computational inefficiency.
AI.method: Two-stage framework: Stage 1 uses Coarse-HADiff with Latent Spatio-Temporal U-Net and dual Drift-Jitter branches to model coarse-grained dependencies; Stage 2 uses Behavior Pattern Extraction, Fine-HADiff, and Semantic Alignment to generate fine-grained traces.
AI.result: SynHAT outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on real-world datasets from four cities across three countries, achieving 52% improvement on spatial metrics and 33% improvement on temporal metrics.
AI.conclusion: SynHAT provides an effective solution for generating realistic, privacy-preserving human activity traces that maintain data utility while addressing computational efficiency challenges.

title: Find the Differences: Differential Morphing Attack Detection vs Face Recognition
summary: Morphing is a challenge to face recognition (FR) for which several morphing attack detection solutions have been proposed. We argue that face recognition and differential morphing attack detection (D-MAD) in principle perform very similar tasks, which we support by comparing an FR system with two existing D-MAD approaches. We also show that currently used decision thresholds inherently lead to FR systems being vulnerable to morphing attacks and that this explains the tradeoff between performance on normal images and vulnerability to morphing attacks. We propose using FR systems that are already in place for morphing detection and introduce a new evaluation threshold that guarantees an upper limit to the vulnerability to morphing attacks - even of unknown types.

AI.tldr: FR systems can be used for morphing attack detection with proper thresholding to guarantee security against unknown attack types.
AI.motivation: Morphing attacks pose a security threat to face recognition systems, and existing differential morphing attack detection (D-MAD) approaches have limitations. The authors argue that FR and D-MAD perform similar tasks and want to leverage existing FR infrastructure for morphing detection.
AI.method: Compare FR systems with existing D-MAD approaches, analyze current decision thresholds, and propose using FR systems for morphing detection with a new evaluation threshold that ensures security against morphing attacks.
AI.result: Current FR thresholds make systems vulnerable to morphing attacks, explaining the tradeoff between normal performance and attack vulnerability. The proposed threshold guarantees an upper limit to vulnerability even for unknown attack types.
AI.conclusion: Existing FR systems can effectively perform morphing attack detection when equipped with appropriate security-guaranteeing thresholds, providing protection against both known and unknown morphing attacks.

title: Gating Enables Curvature: A Geometric Expressivity Gap in Attention
summary: Multiplicative gating is widely used in neural architectures and has recently been applied to attention layers to improve performance and training stability in large language models. Despite the success of gated attention, the mathematical implications of gated attention mechanisms remain poorly understood. We study attention through the geometry of its representations by modeling outputs as mean parameters of Gaussian distributions and analyzing the induced Fisher--Rao geometry. We show that ungated attention operator is restricted to intrinsically flat statistical manifolds due to its affine structure, while multiplicative gating enables non-flat geometries, including positively curved manifolds that are unattainable in the ungated setting. These results establish a geometric expressivity gap between ungated and gated attention. Empirically, we show that gated models exhibit higher representation curvature and improved performance on tasks requiring nonlinear decision boundaries whereas they provide no consistent advantage on tasks with linear decision boundaries. Furthermore, we identify a structured regime in which curvature accumulates under composition, yielding a systematic depth amplification effect.

AI.tldr: Gated attention enables non-flat geometric representations (including positive curvature) that ungated attention cannot achieve, creating a geometric expressivity gap that improves performance on nonlinear tasks.
AI.motivation: Despite the empirical success of gated attention in improving performance and training stability in large language models, the mathematical and geometric implications of these mechanisms remain poorly understood. The paper aims to provide a geometric understanding of why gated attention works better than ungated attention.
AI.method: The authors model attention outputs as mean parameters of Gaussian distributions and analyze the induced Fisher-Rao geometry. They compare ungated attention (which has an affine structure) with multiplicatively gated attention, examining the geometric properties of their statistical manifolds. They also conduct empirical studies to measure representation curvature and performance on tasks with different decision boundary characteristics.
AI.result: Ungated attention is restricted to intrinsically flat statistical manifolds due to its affine structure, while multiplicative gating enables non-flat geometries including positively curved manifolds. This creates a geometric expressivity gap between the two approaches. Empirically, gated models show higher representation curvature and better performance on tasks requiring nonlinear decision boundaries, but no consistent advantage on linear tasks. The authors also identify a structured regime where curvature accumulates under composition, creating a depth amplification effect.
AI.conclusion: Multiplicative gating in attention mechanisms provides geometric expressivity that ungated attention cannot achieve, enabling curved statistical manifolds that are beneficial for tasks requiring nonlinear decision boundaries. This geometric perspective explains the empirical advantages of gated attention and reveals systematic depth amplification effects through curvature accumulation.

title: HWE-Bench: Benchmarking LLM Agents on Real-World Hardware Bug Repair Tasks
summary: Existing benchmarks for hardware design primarily evaluate Large Language Models (LLMs) on isolated, component-level tasks such as generating HDL modules from specifications, leaving repository-scale evaluation unaddressed. We introduce HWE-Bench, the first large-scale, repository-level benchmark for evaluating LLM agents on real-world hardware bug repair tasks. HWE-Bench comprises 417 task instances derived from real historical bug-fix pull requests across six major open-source projects spanning both Verilog/SystemVerilog and Chisel, covering RISC-V cores, SoCs, and security roots-of-trust. Each task is grounded in a fully containerized environment where the agent must resolve a real bug report, with correctness validated through the project's native simulation and regression flows. The benchmark is built through a largely automated pipeline that enables efficient expansion to new repositories. We evaluate seven LLMs with four agent frameworks and find that the best agent resolves 70.7% of tasks overall, with performance exceeding 90% on smaller cores but dropping below 65% on complex SoC-level projects. We observe larger performance gaps across models than commonly reported on software benchmarks, and difficulty is driven by project scope and bug-type distribution rather than code size alone. Our failure analysis traces agent failures to three stages of the debugging process: fault localization, hardware-semantic reasoning, and cross-artifact coordination across RTL, configuration, and verification components, providing concrete directions for developing more capable hardware-aware agents.

AI.tldr: HWE-Bench is the first repository-level benchmark for evaluating LLM agents on real-world hardware bug repair tasks across multiple open-source projects, showing significant performance gaps between models and identifying key failure points in the debugging process.
AI.motivation: Existing hardware design benchmarks only evaluate LLMs on isolated, component-level tasks like generating HDL modules, leaving repository-scale evaluation unaddressed. There's a need for benchmarks that reflect real-world hardware development complexity.
AI.method: Created HWE-Bench with 417 task instances from real historical bug-fix pull requests across 6 major open-source projects (Verilog/SystemVerilog and Chisel). Each task is in a fully containerized environment where agents must resolve real bug reports, with correctness validated through native simulation and regression flows. Built through an automated pipeline for scalability.
AI.result: Best agent resolved 70.7% of tasks overall, with performance exceeding 90% on smaller cores but dropping below 65% on complex SoC-level projects. Larger performance gaps across models than commonly reported on software benchmarks. Difficulty driven by project scope and bug-type distribution rather than code size alone.
AI.conclusion: Failure analysis reveals agent failures occur in three stages: fault localization, hardware-semantic reasoning, and cross-artifact coordination across RTL, configuration, and verification components. This provides concrete directions for developing more capable hardware-aware agents.

title: A Mechanistic Account of Attention Sinks in GPT-2: One Circuit, Broader Implications for Mitigation
summary: Transformers commonly exhibit an attention sink: disproportionately high attention to the first position. We study this behavior in GPT-2-style models with learned query biases and absolute positional embeddings. Combining structural analysis with causal interventions, validated across natural-language, mathematical, and code inputs, we find that the sink arises from the interaction among (i) a learned query bias, (ii) the first-layer MLP transformation of the positional encoding, and (iii) structure in the key projection. Crucially, each component we identify is individually dispensable: architectures omitting each of them robustly exhibit sinks. This indicates that attention sinks may arise through distinct circuits across architectures. These findings inform mitigation of sinks, and motivate broader investigation into why sinks emerge.

AI.tldr: Attention sinks in transformers arise from interactions between learned query biases, first-layer MLP processing of positional encodings, and key projection structure, with each component being individually dispensable.
AI.motivation: To understand why transformers exhibit attention sinks (disproportionately high attention to the first position) and identify the underlying mechanisms that cause this behavior.
AI.method: Combined structural analysis with causal interventions on GPT-2-style models with learned query biases and absolute positional embeddings, validated across natural-language, mathematical, and code inputs.
AI.result: Found that attention sinks arise from interactions among three components: (1) learned query bias, (2) first-layer MLP transformation of positional encoding, and (3) structure in key projection. Each component is individually dispensable, suggesting sinks can emerge through different circuits across architectures.
AI.conclusion: Attention sinks may arise through distinct circuits across different architectures, and these findings can inform mitigation strategies while motivating broader investigation into why sinks emerge in transformer models.

title: SGA-MCTS: Decoupling Planning from Execution via Training-Free Atomic Experience Retrieval
summary: LLM-powered systems require complex multi-step decision-making abilities to solve real-world tasks, yet current planning approaches face a trade-off between the high latency of inference-time search and the limited generalization of supervised fine-tuning. To address this limitation, we introduce \textbf{SGA-MCTS}, a framework that casts LLM planning as non-parametric retrieval. Offline, we leverage Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to explore the solution space and distill high-fidelity trajectories into State-Goal-Action (SGA) atoms. These atoms are de-lexicalized primitives that abstract concrete entities into symbolic slots, preserving reusable causal logic while discarding domain-specific noise. Online, a retrieval-augmented agent employs a hybrid symbolic-semantic mechanism to fetch relevant SGAs and re-ground them into the current context as soft reasoning hints. Empirical results on complex benchmarks demonstrate that this paradigm enables frozen, open-weights models to match the performance of SOTA systems (e.g., GPT-5) without task-specific fine-tuning. By effectively amortizing the heavy computational cost of search, SGA-MCTS achieves System 2 reasoning depth at System 1 inference speeds, rendering autonomous planning both scalable and real-time feasible.

AI.tldr: SGA-MCTS enables LLMs to perform complex planning at inference speed by distilling MCTS search results into reusable symbolic primitives that can be retrieved and grounded during inference.
AI.motivation: Current LLM planning approaches face a trade-off between high-latency inference-time search (slow but generalizable) and limited supervised fine-tuning (fast but not generalizable). There's a need for systems that can perform complex multi-step decision-making at real-time speeds without task-specific training.
AI.method: Offline: Use Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to explore solution space and distill high-quality trajectories into State-Goal-Action (SGA) atoms - de-lexicalized primitives that abstract concrete entities into symbolic slots. Online: Use hybrid symbolic-semantic retrieval to fetch relevant SGAs and re-ground them into current context as soft reasoning hints for a frozen LLM.
AI.result: Empirical results on complex benchmarks show frozen, open-weights models can match performance of SOTA systems (e.g., GPT-5) without task-specific fine-tuning. Achieves System 2 reasoning depth at System 1 inference speeds by amortizing computational cost of search.
AI.conclusion: SGA-MCTS enables autonomous planning that is both scalable and real-time feasible by effectively amortizing heavy computational search costs, allowing frozen models to perform complex reasoning at inference speed through retrieval of distilled symbolic knowledge.

title: ASGNet: Adaptive Spectrum Guidance Network for Automatic Polyp Segmentation
summary: Early identification and removal of polyps can reduce the risk of developing colorectal cancer. However, the diverse morphologies, complex backgrounds and often concealed nature of polyps make polyp segmentation in colonoscopy images highly challenging. Despite the promising performance of existing deep learning-based polyp segmentation methods, their perceptual capabilities remain biased toward local regions, mainly because of the strong spatial correlations between neighboring pixels in the spatial domain. This limitation makes it difficult to capture the complete polyp structures, ultimately leading to sub-optimal segmentation results. In this paper, we propose a novel adaptive spectrum guidance network, called ASGNet, which addresses the limitations of spatial perception by integrating spectral features with global attributes. Specifically, we first design a spectrum-guided non-local perception module that jointly aggregates local and global information, therefore enhancing the discriminability of polyp structures, and refining their boundaries. Moreover, we introduce a multi-source semantic extractor that integrates rich high-level semantic information to assist in the preliminary localization of polyps.
