Specifically, FEDSNet utilizes the Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) and a low-pass filtering mechanism to explicitly isolate low-frequency global structural components from spatial features, thereby suppressing background interference. Truncated Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) is employed to construct independent, low-rank linear subspaces for both spatial texture and frequency structural features. An adaptive gating mechanism is designed to dynamically fuse the projection distances from these dual views. This strategy leverages the structural stability of the frequency subspace to prevent the spatial subspace from overfitting to background features. Extensive experiments on four benchmark datasets - CUB-200-2011, Stanford Cars, Stanford Dogs, and FGVC-Aircraft - demonstrate that FEDSNet exhibits excellent classification performance and robustness, achieving highly competitive results compared to existing metric learning algorithms. Complexity analysis further confirms that the proposed network achieves a favorable balance between high accuracy and computational efficiency, providing an effective new paradigm for few-shot fine-grained visual recognition.

AI.tldr: FEDSNet improves few-shot fine-grained classification by combining spatial and frequency domain features to reduce texture bias and background noise, achieving better performance and robustness.
AI.motivation: Existing metric learning methods for few-shot fine-grained classification rely only on spatial features, which have inherent texture biases that entangle structural details with background noise. Single-view metrics also lack cross-view geometric constraints, causing overfitting to noise and structural instability.
AI.method: Proposes Frequency-Enhanced Dual-Subspace Network (FEDSNet) that: 1) Uses DCT and low-pass filtering to isolate low-frequency structural components from spatial features, 2) Applies truncated SVD to construct independent low-rank subspaces for spatial texture and frequency structural features, 3) Designs adaptive gating mechanism to dynamically fuse projection distances from dual views.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on four benchmark datasets (CUB-200-2011, Stanford Cars, Stanford Dogs, FGVC-Aircraft) show excellent classification performance and robustness, achieving highly competitive results compared to existing metric learning algorithms. Complexity analysis confirms favorable balance between accuracy and computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: FEDSNet provides an effective new paradigm for few-shot fine-grained visual recognition by leveraging structural stability of frequency subspace to prevent spatial subspace from overfitting to background features, addressing texture bias and noise issues in existing methods.

title: What Is the Minimum Architecture for Prolepsis? Early Irrevocable Commitment Across Tasks in Small Transformers
comment: 24 pages, 3 figures. Under review at COLM 2026. Independent replication of the rhyme-planning finding from Lindsey et al. (2025) on open-weights models; extended to factual recall
summary: When do transformers commit to a decision, and what prevents them from correcting it? We introduce \textbf{prolepsis}: a transformer commits early, task-specific attention heads sustain the commitment, and no layer corrects it. Replicating \citeauthor{lindsey2025biology}'s (\citeyear{lindsey2025biology}) planning-site finding on open models (Gemma~2 2B, Llama~3.2 1B), we ask five questions. (Q1)~Planning is invisible to six residual-stream methods; CLTs are necessary. (Q2)~The planning-site spike replicates with identical geometry. (Q3)~Specific attention heads route the decision to the output, filling a gap flagged as invisible to attribution graphs. (Q4)~Search requires ${\leq}16$ layers; commitment requires more. (Q5)~Factual recall shows the same motif at a different network depth, with zero overlap between recurring planning heads and the factual top-10. Prolepsis is architectural: the template is shared, the routing substrates differ. All experiments run on a single consumer GPU (16\,GB VRAM).

AI.tldr: Transformers commit to decisions early via "prolepsis" - specific attention heads sustain commitments that no layer corrects, as shown in planning and factual recall tasks.
AI.motivation: To understand when transformers commit to decisions and what mechanisms prevent them from correcting those commitments, examining the phenomenon called "prolepsis."
AI.method: Replicated Lindsey et al.'s planning-site finding on open models (Gemma 2 2B, Llama 3.2 1B) using five research questions to analyze decision commitment mechanisms.
AI.result: Planning invisible to residual-stream methods; CLTs needed. Planning-site spike replicates with identical geometry. Specific attention heads route decisions to output. Search requires ≤16 layers; commitment requires more. Factual recall shows same motif at different depth with zero overlap between planning and factual heads.
AI.conclusion: Prolepsis is architectural: the template is shared across tasks, but routing substrates differ. Early commitment is sustained by task-specific attention heads with no correction mechanisms.

title: Governing Reflective Human-AI Collaboration: A Framework for Epistemic Scaffolding and Traceable Reasoning
summary:
Large language models have advanced rapidly, from pattern recognition to emerging forms of reasoning, yet they remain confined to linguistic simulation rather than grounded understanding. They can produce fluent outputs that resemble reflection, but lack temporal continuity, causal feedback, and anchoring in real-world interaction. This paper proposes a complementary approach in which reasoning is treated as a relational process distributed between human and model rather than an internal capability of either.
  Building on recent work on "System-2" learning, we relocate reflective reasoning to the interaction layer. Instead of engineering reasoning solely within models, we frame it as a cognitive protocol that can be structured, measured, and governed using existing systems. This perspective emphasizes collaborative intelligence, combining human judgment and contextual understanding with machine speed, memory, and associative capacity.
  We introduce "The Architect's Pen" as a practical method. Like an architect who thinks through drawing, the human uses the model as an external medium for structured reflection. By embedding phases of articulation, critique, and revision into human-AI interaction, the dialogue itself becomes a reasoning loop: human abstraction -> model articulation -> human reflection.
  This reframes the question from whether the model can think to whether the human-AI system can reason. The framework enables auditable reasoning traces and supports alignment with emerging governance standards, including the EU AI Act and ISO/IEC 42001. It provides a practical path toward more transparent, controllable, and accountable AI use without requiring new model architectures.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes reframing reasoning as a collaborative human-AI process rather than an internal model capability, introducing "The Architect's Pen" method where human and AI engage in structured reflection loops.
AI.motivation: Current LLMs produce fluent outputs but lack true understanding, temporal continuity, causal feedback, and real-world grounding. They simulate reasoning rather than genuinely reason, creating a need for approaches that combine human judgment with AI capabilities.
AI.method: Introduces "The Architect's Pen" method where reasoning is treated as a relational process between human and model. Human uses AI as an external medium for structured reflection through phases of articulation, critique, and revision, creating a reasoning loop: human abstraction → model articulation → human reflection.
AI.result: Reframes reasoning from an internal model capability to a collaborative human-AI system capability. Enables auditable reasoning traces and supports alignment with governance standards like EU AI Act and ISO/IEC 42001 without requiring new model architectures.
AI.conclusion: Shifts focus from whether models can think to whether human-AI systems can reason, providing a practical path toward more transparent, controllable, and accountable AI use through collaborative intelligence frameworks.

title: UniDoc-RL: Coarse-to-Fine Visual RAG with Hierarchical Actions and Dense Rewards
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) extends Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) with external visual knowledge. However, existing visual RAG systems typically rely on generic retrieval signals that overlook the fine-grained visual semantics essential for complex reasoning. To address this limitation, we propose UniDoc-RL, a unified reinforcement learning framework in which an LVLM agent jointly performs retrieval, reranking, active visual perception, and reasoning. UniDoc-RL formulates visual information acquisition as a sequential decision-making problem with a hierarchical action space. Specifically, it progressively refines visual evidence from coarse-grained document retrieval to fine-grained image selection and active region cropping, allowing the model to suppress irrelevant content and attend to information-dense regions. For effective end-to-end training, we introduce a dense multi-reward scheme that provides task-aware supervision for each action. Based on Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), UniDoc-RL aligns agent behavior with multiple objectives without relying on a separate value network. To support this training paradigm, we curate a comprehensive dataset of high-quality reasoning trajectories with fine-grained action annotations. Experiments on three benchmarks demonstrate that UniDoc-RL consistently surpasses state-of-the-art baselines, yielding up to 17.7% gains over prior RL-based methods.

AI.tldr: UniDoc-RL is a unified reinforcement learning framework that enables LVLMs to jointly perform retrieval, reranking, active visual perception, and reasoning through hierarchical actions, outperforming prior methods by up to 17.7%.
AI.motivation: Existing visual RAG systems rely on generic retrieval signals that overlook fine-grained visual semantics needed for complex reasoning, limiting their effectiveness in detailed visual understanding tasks.
AI.method: Proposes UniDoc-RL, a unified RL framework with hierarchical action space: document retrieval → image selection → active region cropping. Uses Group Relative Policy Optimization with dense multi-reward scheme for end-to-end training without separate value network.
AI.result: Experiments on three benchmarks show UniDoc-RL consistently surpasses state-of-the-art baselines, achieving up to 17.7% gains over prior RL-based methods.
AI.conclusion: UniDoc-RL effectively addresses limitations of existing visual RAG systems by enabling fine-grained visual semantics acquisition through sequential decision-making and hierarchical actions, demonstrating superior performance in visual reasoning tasks.

title: DLink: Distilling Layer-wise and Dominant Knowledge from EEG Foundation Models
summary: EEG foundation models (FMs) achieve strong cross-subject and cross-task generalization but impose substantial computational and memory costs that hinder deployment on embedded BCI systems. Knowledge distillation is a natural solution; however, conventional methods fail for EEG FMs because task-relevant semantics are often distributed across intermediate layers, and aggressive dimensionality reduction can distort oscillatory structure via representational collapse and aliasing. To address these challenges, we propose DLink (Distilling Layer-wise and Dominant Knowledge), a unified framework for transferring knowledge from large EEG FMs to compact students with three key innovations: (1) a dynamic Router that adaptively aggregates teacher layers to capture dominant intermediate representations; (2) an EEG MiC student with a Mimic-then-Compress pipeline, which inherits high-dimensional teacher features and then applies structured spatio-temporal compression to avoid a heavy classification head; and (3) spectral distillation that aligns teacher-student representations in the frequency domain to regularize compression and mitigate aliasing and temporal jitter. Experiments on four EEG benchmarks show that DLink enables compact students to outperform lightweight baselines while approaching fully fine-tuned FM performance at substantially lower model size and inference cost.

AI.tldr: DLink is a knowledge distillation framework that transfers knowledge from large EEG foundation models to compact student models using layer-wise aggregation, structured compression, and spectral alignment to maintain oscillatory structure while reducing computational costs.
AI.motivation: EEG foundation models achieve strong generalization but are computationally expensive for embedded BCI systems. Conventional knowledge distillation fails for EEG FMs because task-relevant semantics are distributed across layers, and aggressive compression distorts oscillatory structure via representational collapse and aliasing.
AI.method: DLink framework with three key innovations: (1) dynamic Router that adaptively aggregates teacher layers to capture dominant intermediate representations; (2) EEG MiC student with Mimic-then-Compress pipeline that inherits high-dimensional teacher features then applies structured spatio-temporal compression; (3) spectral distillation that aligns teacher-student representations in frequency domain to regularize compression and mitigate aliasing/temporal jitter.
AI.result: Experiments on four EEG benchmarks show DLink enables compact students to outperform lightweight baselines while approaching fully fine-tuned FM performance at substantially lower model size and inference cost.
AI.conclusion: DLink provides an effective knowledge distillation framework for EEG foundation models that maintains performance while dramatically reducing computational requirements, enabling deployment on embedded BCI systems.

title: Flow of Truth: Proactive Temporal Forensics for Image-to-Video Generation
summary: The rapid rise of image-to-video (I2V) generation enables realistic videos to be created from a single image but also brings new forensic demands. Unlike static images, I2V content evolves over time, requiring forensics to move beyond 2D pixel-level tampering localization toward tracing how pixels flow and transform throughout the video. As frames progress, embedded traces drift and deform, making traditional spatial forensics ineffective. To address this unexplored dimension, we present **Flow of Truth**, the first proactive framework focusing on temporal forensics in I2V generation. A key challenge lies in discovering a forensic signature that can evolve consistently with the generation process, which is inherently a creative transformation rather than a deterministic reconstruction. Despite this intrinsic difficulty, we innovatively redefine video generation as *the motion of pixels through time rather than the synthesis of frames*. Building on this view, we propose a learnable forensic template that follows pixel motion and a template-guided flow module that decouples motion from image content, enabling robust temporal tracing. Experiments show that Flow of Truth generalizes across commercial and open-source I2V models, substantially improving temporal forensics performance.

AI.tldr: Flow of Truth is the first proactive framework for temporal forensics in image-to-video generation, focusing on tracing pixel flow and transformation over time rather than static 2D tampering detection.
AI.motivation: Image-to-video generation creates realistic videos from single images but introduces new forensic challenges. Unlike static images, I2V content evolves over time, requiring forensics to trace how pixels flow and transform throughout the video. Traditional spatial forensics are ineffective as embedded traces drift and deform across frames.
AI.method: Redefines video generation as "the motion of pixels through time rather than the synthesis of frames." Proposes a learnable forensic template that follows pixel motion and a template-guided flow module that decouples motion from image content, enabling robust temporal tracing.
AI.result: Experiments show that Flow of Truth generalizes across commercial and open-source I2V models and substantially improves temporal forensics performance.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully addresses the unexplored dimension of temporal forensics in I2V generation by focusing on pixel motion rather than static frame analysis, providing a robust solution for tracing how content evolves over time in generated videos.

title: When Fairness Metrics Disagree: Evaluating the Reliability of Demographic Fairness Assessment in Machine Learning
summary: The evaluation of fairness in machine learning systems has become a central concern in high-stakes applications, including biometric recognition, healthcare decision-making, and automated risk assessment. Existing approaches typically rely on a small number of fairness metrics to assess model behaviour across group partitions, implicitly assuming that these metrics provide consistent and reliable conclusions. However, different fairness metrics capture distinct statistical properties of model performance and may therefore produce conflicting assessments when applied to the same system. In this work, we investigate the consistency of fairness evaluation by conducting a systematic multi-metric analysis of demographic bias in machine learning models. Using face recognition as a controlled experimental setting, we evaluate model performance across multiple group partitions under a range of commonly used fairness metrics, including error-rate disparities and performance-based measures. Our results demonstrate that fairness assessments can vary significantly depending on the choice of metrics, leading to contradictory conclusions regarding model bias. To quantify this phenomenon, we introduce the Fairness Disagreement Index (FDI), a measure designed to capture the degree of inconsistency across fairness metrics. We further show that disagreement remains high across thresholds and model configurations. These findings highlight a critical limitation in current fairness evaluation practices and suggest that single-metric reporting is insufficient for reliable bias assessment.

AI.tldr: Fairness assessments in ML vary significantly across different metrics, leading to contradictory conclusions about model bias, highlighting the insufficiency of single-metric reporting.
AI.motivation: Current fairness evaluation practices rely on a small number of metrics assuming consistent conclusions, but different metrics capture distinct statistical properties and may produce conflicting assessments of the same system.
AI.method: Systematic multi-metric analysis of demographic bias using face recognition as experimental setting, evaluating across multiple group partitions under various fairness metrics (error-rate disparities, performance-based measures). Introduced Fairness Disagreement Index (FDI) to quantify inconsistency across metrics.
AI.result: Fairness assessments vary significantly depending on metric choice, leading to contradictory conclusions about model bias. Disagreement remains high across thresholds and model configurations.
AI.conclusion: Single-metric reporting is insufficient for reliable bias assessment. Current fairness evaluation practices have critical limitations due to metric inconsistency.

title: Dual-Axis Generative Reward Model Toward Semantic and Turn-taking Robustness in Interactive Spoken Dialogue Models
summary: Achieving seamless, human-like interaction remains a key challenge for full-duplex spoken dialogue models (SDMs). Reinforcement learning (RL) has substantially enhanced text- and vision-language models, while well-designed reward signals are crucial for the performance of RL. We consider RL a promising strategy to address the key challenge for SDMs. However, a fundamental barrier persists: prevailing automated metrics for assessing interaction quality rely on superficial proxies, such as behavioral statistics or timing-prediction accuracy, failing to provide reliable reward signals for RL. On the other hand, human evaluations, despite their richness, remain costly, inconsistent, and difficult to scale. We tackle this critical barrier by proposing a Dual-Axis Generative Reward Model, which is trained to understand complex interaction dynamics using a detailed taxonomy and an annotated dataset, produces a single score and, crucially, provides separate evaluations for semantic quality and interaction timing. Such dual outputs furnish precise diagnostic feedback for SDMs and deliver a dependable, instructive reward signal suitable for online reinforcement learning. Our model achieves state-of-the-art performance on interaction-quality assessment across a wide spectrum of datasets, spanning synthetic dialogues and complex real-world interactions.

AI.tldr: A dual-axis generative reward model for spoken dialogue systems that provides separate semantic quality and interaction timing scores to enable effective reinforcement learning.
AI.motivation: Current automated metrics for spoken dialogue interaction quality are superficial (behavioral stats, timing-prediction accuracy) and don't provide reliable RL rewards, while human evaluations are costly and inconsistent. There's a need for better reward signals to enable RL for improving human-like interaction in full-duplex spoken dialogue models.
AI.method: Proposes a Dual-Axis Generative Reward Model trained with a detailed taxonomy and annotated dataset to understand complex interaction dynamics. The model produces a single overall score and crucially provides separate evaluations for semantic quality and interaction timing.
AI.result: The model achieves state-of-the-art performance on interaction-quality assessment across diverse datasets including synthetic dialogues and complex real-world interactions.
AI.conclusion: The dual-axis reward model provides precise diagnostic feedback for spoken dialogue models and delivers dependable, instructive reward signals suitable for online reinforcement learning, addressing the critical barrier to using RL for improving human-like interaction in spoken dialogue systems.

title: Quality-Aware Calibration for AI-Generated Image Detection in the Wild
comment: Accepted at the APAI Workshop at CVPR 2026
summary: Significant progress has been made in detecting synthetic images, however most existing approaches operate on a single image instance and overlook a key characteristic of real-world dissemination: as viral images circulate on the web, multiple near-duplicate versions appear and lose quality due to repeated operations like recompression, resizing and cropping. As a consequence, the same image may yield inconsistent forensic predictions based on which version has been analyzed. In this work, to address this issue we propose QuAD (Quality-Aware calibration with near-Duplicates) a novel framework that makes decisions based on all available near-duplicates of the same image. Given a query, we retrieve its online near-duplicates and feed them to a detector: the resulting scores are then aggregated based on the estimated quality of the corresponding instance. By doing so, we take advantage of all pieces of information while accounting for the reduced reliability of images impaired by multiple processing steps. To support large-scale evaluation, we introduce two datasets: AncesTree, an in-lab dataset of 136k images organized in stochastic degradation trees that simulate online reposting dynamics, and ReWIND, a real-world dataset of nearly 10k near-duplicate images collected from viral web content. Experiments on several state-of-the-art detectors show that our quality-aware fusion improves their performance consistently, with an average gain of around 8% in terms of balanced accuracy compared to plain average. Our results highlight the importance of jointly processing all the images available online to achieve reliable detection of AI-generated content in real-world applications. Code and data are publicly available at https://grip-unina.github.io/QuAD/

AI.tldr: QuAD framework improves synthetic image detection by aggregating predictions from multiple near-duplicate versions of viral images, accounting for quality degradation from online reposting.
AI.motivation: Existing synthetic image detectors analyze single images, ignoring that viral images circulate online as multiple near-duplicates with varying quality due to repeated operations like recompression, resizing, and cropping, leading to inconsistent forensic predictions.
AI.method: QuAD retrieves online near-duplicates of a query image, feeds them to a detector, and aggregates the resulting scores based on estimated quality of each instance, giving less weight to images impaired by multiple processing steps.
AI.result: Quality-aware fusion improves performance of several state-of-the-art detectors by average 8% balanced accuracy compared to plain averaging. Two datasets introduced: AncesTree (136k images with degradation trees) and ReWIND (10k real-world near-duplicates).
AI.conclusion: Jointly processing all available near-duplicate images online is crucial for reliable AI-generated content detection in real-world applications, and quality-aware aggregation significantly improves detection consistency.

title: No More Guessing: a Verifiable Gradient Inversion Attack in Federated Learning
summary:
Gradient inversion attacks threaten client privacy in federated learning by reconstructing training samples from clients' shared gradients. Gradients aggregate contributions from multiple records and existing attacks may fail to disentangle them, yielding incorrect reconstructions with no intrinsic way to certify success. In vision and language, attackers may fall back on human inspection to judge reconstruction plausibility, but this is far less feasible for numerical tabular records, fueling the impression that tabular data is less vulnerable.
  We challenge this perception by proposing a verifiable gradient inversion attack (VGIA) that provides an explicit certificate of correctness for reconstructed samples. Our method adopts a geometric view of ReLU leakage: the activation boundary of a fully connected layer defines a hyperplane in input space. VGIA introduces an algebraic, subspace-based verification test that detects when a hyperplane-delimited region contains exactly one record. Once isolation is certified, VGIA recovers the corresponding feature vector analytically and reconstructs the target via a lightweight optimization step.
  Experiments on tabular benchmarks with large batch sizes demonstrate exact record and target recovery in regimes where existing state-of-the-art attacks either fail or cannot assess reconstruction fidelity. Compared to prior geometric approaches, VGIA allocates hyperplane queries more effectively, yielding faster reconstructions with fewer attack rounds.

AI.tldr: VGIA is a verifiable gradient inversion attack for tabular data that provides certificates of correctness for reconstructed samples, challenging the perception that tabular data is less vulnerable than vision/language data.
AI.motivation: Existing gradient inversion attacks struggle with tabular data because they can't disentangle contributions from multiple records and lack intrinsic verification methods. While vision/language attacks can rely on human inspection, this isn't feasible for numerical tabular data, creating a false sense of security.
AI.method: VGIA uses a geometric approach based on ReLU leakage: activation boundaries of fully connected layers define hyperplanes in input space. It introduces an algebraic subspace-based verification test to detect when a hyperplane-delimited region contains exactly one record. Once isolation is certified, it recovers the feature vector analytically and reconstructs the target via lightweight optimization.
AI.result: Experiments on tabular benchmarks with large batch sizes show exact record and target recovery where state-of-the-art attacks either fail or can't assess reconstruction fidelity. VGIA allocates hyperplane queries more effectively than prior geometric approaches, yielding faster reconstructions with fewer attack rounds.
AI.conclusion: Tabular data is more vulnerable to gradient inversion attacks than previously thought. VGIA provides verifiable reconstruction with certificates of correctness, challenging the perception that tabular data is inherently safer than vision/language data in federated learning.

title: WavAlign: Enhancing Intelligence and Expressiveness in Spoken Dialogue Models via Adaptive Hybrid Post-Training
summary: End-to-end spoken dialogue models have garnered significant attention because they offer a higher potential ceiling in expressiveness and perceptual ability than cascaded systems. However, the intelligence and expressiveness of current open-source spoken dialogue models often remain below expectations. Motivated by the success of online reinforcement learning(RL) in other domains, one might attempt to directly apply preference optimization to spoken dialogue models, yet this transfer is non-trivial. We analyze these obstacles from the perspectives of reward modeling and rollout sampling, focusing on how sparse preference supervision interacts with dense speech generation under shared-parameter updates. Based on the analysis, we propose a modality-aware adaptive post-training recipe that makes RL practical for spoken dialogue: it constrains preference updates to the semantic channel and improves acoustic behavior via explicit anchoring, while dynamically regulating their mixture from rollout statistics to avoid unreliable preference gradients. We evaluate the method across multiple spoken dialogue benchmarks and representative architectures, and observe consistent improvements in semantic quality and speech expressiveness.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a modality-aware adaptive post-training recipe to make reinforcement learning practical for spoken dialogue models, addressing challenges in applying preference optimization to speech generation.
AI.motivation: Current open-source spoken dialogue models often fall short in intelligence and expressiveness despite end-to-end approaches having higher potential. Direct application of online reinforcement learning from other domains to spoken dialogue is non-trivial due to challenges with sparse preference supervision interacting with dense speech generation under shared-parameter updates.
AI.method: A modality-aware adaptive post-training recipe that: 1) constrains preference updates to the semantic channel, 2) improves acoustic behavior via explicit anchoring, and 3) dynamically regulates their mixture from rollout statistics to avoid unreliable preference gradients.
AI.result: The method shows consistent improvements in semantic quality and speech expressiveness across multiple spoken dialogue benchmarks and representative architectures.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach successfully makes reinforcement learning practical for spoken dialogue models by addressing the specific challenges of applying preference optimization to speech generation, leading to improved performance in both semantic and acoustic dimensions.

title: Implicit Neural Representations: A Signal Processing Perspective
summary: Implicit neural representations (INRs) mark a fundamental shift in signal modeling, moving from discrete sampled data to continuous functional representations. By parameterizing signals as neural networks, INRs provide a unified framework for representing images, audio, video, 3D geometry, and beyond as continuous functions of their coordinates. This functional viewpoint enables signal operations such as differentiation to be carried out analytically through automatic differentiation rather than through discrete approximations. In this article, we examine the evolution of INRs from a signal processing perspective, emphasizing spectral behavior, sampling theory, and multiscale representation. We trace the progression from standard coordinate based networks, which exhibit a spectral bias toward low frequency components, to more advanced designs that reshape the approximation space through specialized activations, including periodic, localized, and adaptive functions. We also discuss structured representations, such as hierarchical decompositions and hash grid encodings, that improve spatial adaptivity and computational efficiency. We further highlight the utility of INRs across a broad range of applications, including inverse problems in medical and radar imaging, compression, and 3D scene representation. By interpreting INRs as learned signal models whose approximation spaces adapt to the underlying data, this article clarifies the field's core conceptual developments and outlines open challenges in theoretical stability, weight space interpretability, and large scale generalization.

AI.tldr: INRs represent signals as neural networks for continuous functional modeling, enabling analytical operations and applications across imaging, compression, and 3D representation.
AI.motivation: To provide a unified continuous functional framework for signal representation that enables analytical operations through automatic differentiation rather than discrete approximations.
AI.method: Parameterizing signals as neural networks, progressing from standard coordinate-based networks to advanced designs with specialized activations (periodic, localized, adaptive) and structured representations (hierarchical decompositions, hash grid encodings).
AI.result: INRs enable continuous signal modeling with improved spectral behavior, spatial adaptivity, and computational efficiency, applicable to inverse problems in medical/radar imaging, compression, and 3D scene representation.
AI.conclusion: INRs represent learned signal models with adaptive approximation spaces, offering a fundamental shift in signal processing but facing open challenges in theoretical stability, weight space interpretability, and large-scale generalization.

title: Beyond the Laplacian: Doubly Stochastic Matrices for Graph Neural Networks
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) conventionally rely on standard Laplacian or adjacency matrices for structural message passing. In this work, we substitute the traditional Laplacian with a Doubly Stochastic graph Matrix (DSM), derived from the inverse of the modified Laplacian, to naturally encode continuous multi-hop proximity and strict local centrality. To overcome the intractable $O(n^3)$ complexity of exact matrix inversion, we first utilize a truncated Neumann series to scalably approximate the DSM, which serves as the foundation for our proposed DsmNet. Furthermore, because algebraic truncation inherently causes probability mass leakage, we introduce DsmNet-compensate. This variant features a mathematically rigorous Residual Mass Compensation mechanism that analytically re-injects the truncated tail mass into self-loops, strictly restoring row-stochasticity and structural dominance. Extensive theoretical and empirical analyses demonstrate that our decoupled architectures operate efficiently in $O(K|E|)$ time and effectively mitigate over-smoothing by bounding Dirichlet energy decay, providing robust empirical validation on homophilic benchmarks. Finally, we establish the theoretical boundaries of the DSM on heterophilic topologies and demonstrate its versatility as a continuous structural encoding for Graph Transformers.

AI.tldr: DsmNet replaces standard Laplacian with Doubly Stochastic Matrix (DSM) for better graph representation, using scalable approximation and compensation for mass leakage.
AI.motivation: Standard GNNs use Laplacian/adjacency matrices which may not optimally encode multi-hop proximity and local centrality. The paper aims to improve structural message passing with a more expressive doubly stochastic representation.
AI.method: 1) Replace Laplacian with Doubly Stochastic Matrix (DSM) from inverse of modified Laplacian; 2) Use truncated Neumann series for scalable O(K|E|) approximation; 3) Introduce DsmNet-compensate with Residual Mass Compensation to restore row-stochasticity after truncation.
AI.result: The method achieves efficient O(K|E|) computation, mitigates over-smoothing by bounding Dirichlet energy decay, shows strong performance on homophilic benchmarks, and demonstrates versatility for Graph Transformers.
AI.conclusion: DSM provides superior structural encoding for GNNs, offering continuous multi-hop proximity, strict local centrality, scalability, and theoretical guarantees against over-smoothing, with applications extending to heterophilic graphs and Graph Transformers.

title: Discovering Novel LLM Experts via Task-Capability Coevolution
summary: Frontier model developers aim to train models continually to possess emergent, diverse capabilities. To extend capabilities, the current pre-training and post-training paradigm requires manually starting training runs with static datasets or reward functions every time. Addressing this limitation, our work pursues the insight that open-endedness (via the coevolution of models and tasks) can discover models with increasingly novel skills in a single run. We introduce a new model development framework that extends coevolution to large language model (LLM) discovery, open-ended \textit{Assessment Coevolving with Diverse Capabilities} (AC/DC). AC/DC evolves both LLMs via model merging and natural language tasks via synthetic data generation. AC/DC discovers growing archives of LLMs that surpass the capabilities of larger LLMs while taking up less GPU memory. In particular, our LLM populations achieve a broader Coverage of expertise than other curated models or baselines on downstream benchmarks, without \textit{any} explicit benchmark optimization. Furthermore, AC/DC improves Coverage over time, continually innovates on tasks and models, and improves performance in multi-agent best-of-N selection. Our findings highlight the potential of coevolution as a means of discovering broader sets of capabilities from base LLMs. Overall, AC/DC brings us one step closer to a profoundly new paradigm of LLM development, where continual improvements to the diversity of model capabilities can be accelerated by leveraging existing models as stepping stones to increasingly powerful models.

AI.tldr: AC/DC is an open-ended coevolution framework that simultaneously evolves LLMs and natural language tasks to discover increasingly novel capabilities without manual intervention.
AI.motivation: Current LLM development requires manual training runs with static datasets, limiting continuous capability discovery. The authors aim to enable automatic, open-ended discovery of diverse skills through coevolution of models and tasks.
AI.method: AC/DC coevolves LLMs through model merging and evolves natural language tasks via synthetic data generation. It maintains growing archives of models and tasks that mutually improve each other in a continuous loop.
AI.result: Discovered LLM populations surpass larger models' capabilities while using less GPU memory, achieve broader expertise coverage than curated models without benchmark optimization, and continually innovate on tasks and models over time.
AI.conclusion: Coevolution enables automatic discovery of diverse capabilities from base LLMs, representing a paradigm shift toward continual, open-ended LLM development where existing models serve as stepping stones to increasingly powerful ones.

title: Attention-Gated Convolutional Networks for Scanner-Agnostic Quality Assessment
summary: Motion artifacts present a significant challenge in structural MRI (sMRI), often compromising clinical diagnostics and large-scale automated analysis. While manual quality control (QC) remains the gold standard, it is increasingly unscalable for massive longitudinal studies. To address this, we propose a hybrid CNN-Attention framework designed for robust, site-invariant MRI quality assessment. Our architecture integrates a hierarchical 2D CNN encoder for local spatial feature extraction with a multi-head cross-attention mechanism to model global dependencies. This synergy enables the model to prioritize motion relevant artifact signatures, such as ringing and blurring, while dynamically filtering out site-specific intensity variations and background noise. The framework was trained end-to-end on the MR-ART dataset using a balanced cohort of 200 subjects. Performance was evaluated across two tiers: Seen Site Evaluation on a held-out MR-ART partition and Unseen Site Evaluation using 200 subjects from 17 heterogeneous sites in the ABIDE archive. On seen sites, the model achieved a scan-level accuracy of 0.9920 and an F1-score of 0.9919. Crucially, it maintained strong generalization across unseen ABIDE sites (Acc = 0.755) without any retraining or fine-tuning, demonstrating high resilience to domain shift. These results indicate that attention-based feature re-weighting successfully captures universal artifact descriptors, bridging the performance gap between diverse imaging environments and scanner manufacturers.

AI.tldr: Hybrid CNN-Attention framework for robust, site-invariant MRI quality assessment that maintains strong generalization across unseen sites without retraining.
AI.motivation: Motion artifacts in structural MRI compromise clinical diagnostics and automated analysis. Manual quality control is unscalable for massive longitudinal studies, creating a need for automated, robust quality assessment that works across different imaging sites.
AI.method: Proposed hybrid CNN-Attention framework integrating hierarchical 2D CNN encoder for local spatial feature extraction with multi-head cross-attention mechanism to model global dependencies. This enables prioritizing motion-relevant artifact signatures while filtering out site-specific variations and noise. Trained end-to-end on MR-ART dataset with 200 subjects.
AI.result: Achieved scan-level accuracy of 0.9920 and F1-score of 0.9919 on seen sites (MR-ART). Maintained strong generalization across 17 unseen ABIDE sites with accuracy of 0.755 without any retraining or fine-tuning, demonstrating high resilience to domain shift.
AI.conclusion: Attention-based feature re-weighting successfully captures universal artifact descriptors, bridging performance gap between diverse imaging environments and scanner manufacturers. The framework shows promise for scalable, automated MRI quality assessment across heterogeneous clinical settings.

title: FedIDM: Achieving Fast and Stable Convergence in Byzantine Federated Learning through Iterative Distribution Matching
summary: Most existing Byzantine-robust federated learning (FL) methods suffer from slow and unstable convergence. Moreover, when handling a substantial proportion of colluded malicious clients, achieving robustness typically entails compromising model utility. To address these issues, this work introduces FedIDM, which employs distribution matching to construct trustworthy condensed data for identifying and filtering abnormal clients. FedIDM consists of two main components: (1) attack-tolerant condensed data generation, and (2) robust aggregation with negative contribution-based rejection. These components exclude local updates that (1) deviate from the update direction derived from condensed data, or (2) cause a significant loss on the condensed dataset. Comprehensive evaluations on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that FedIDM achieves fast and stable convergence while maintaining acceptable model utility, under multiple state-of-the-art Byzantine attacks involving a large number of malicious clients.

AI.tldr: FedIDM is a Byzantine-robust federated learning method that uses distribution matching to create condensed data for identifying and filtering malicious clients, achieving fast convergence while maintaining model utility under high proportions of malicious clients.
AI.motivation: Existing Byzantine-robust FL methods suffer from slow/unstable convergence and trade-off between robustness and model utility when handling many colluded malicious clients.
AI.method: FedIDM uses distribution matching to construct trustworthy condensed data, with two components: (1) attack-tolerant condensed data generation, and (2) robust aggregation with negative contribution-based rejection that filters updates deviating from condensed data direction or causing significant loss on condensed dataset.
AI.result: Comprehensive evaluations on three benchmark datasets show FedIDM achieves fast and stable convergence while maintaining acceptable model utility under multiple state-of-the-art Byzantine attacks with large numbers of malicious clients.
AI.conclusion: FedIDM effectively addresses convergence and utility issues in Byzantine-robust FL by using distribution matching for condensed data to identify and filter malicious clients, outperforming existing methods in challenging attack scenarios.

title: Hybrid Decision Making via Conformal VLM-generated Guidance
summary: Building on recent advances in AI, hybrid decision making (HDM) holds the promise of improving human decision quality and reducing cognitive load. We work in the context of learning to guide (LtG), a recently proposed HDM framework in which the human is always responsible for the final decision: rather than suggesting decisions, in LtG the AI supplies (textual) guidance useful for facilitating decision making. One limiting factor of existing approaches is that their guidance compounds information about all possible outcomes, and as a result it can be difficult to digest. We address this issue by introducing ConfGuide, a novel LtG approach that generates more succinct and targeted guidance. To this end, it employs conformal risk control to select a set of outcomes, ensuring a cap on the false negative rate. We demonstrate our approach on a real-world multi-label medical diagnosis task. Our empirical evaluation highlights the promise of ConfGuide.

AI.tldr: ConfGuide is a novel AI-guided decision making approach that generates more succinct and targeted guidance using conformal risk control to select outcome sets while ensuring a cap on false negative rates.
AI.motivation: Existing learning-to-guide (LtG) approaches compound information about all possible outcomes, making guidance difficult to digest and limiting their effectiveness in improving human decision quality and reducing cognitive load.
AI.method: ConfGuide employs conformal risk control to select a set of outcomes, ensuring a cap on the false negative rate, which generates more succinct and targeted textual guidance for human decision makers.
AI.result: The approach was demonstrated on a real-world multi-label medical diagnosis task, with empirical evaluation highlighting the promise of ConfGuide for improving decision making.
AI.conclusion: ConfGuide addresses the limitation of existing LtG approaches by providing more digestible guidance through outcome selection with controlled false negative rates, showing promise for practical HDM applications.

title: Learning Where to Embed: Noise-Aware Positional Embedding for Query Retrieval in Small-Object Detection
comment: Accepted to ACM ICMR 2026; 14 pages, 6 figures, and 4 tables
summary: Transformer-based detectors have advanced small-object detection, but they often remain inefficient and vulnerable to background-induced query noise, which motivates deep decoders to refine low-quality queries. We present HELP (Heatmap-guided Embedding Learning Paradigm), a noise-aware positional-semantic fusion framework that studies where to embed positional information by selectively preserving positional encodings in foreground-salient regions while suppressing background clutter. Within HELP, we introduce Heatmap-guided Positional Embedding (HPE) as the core embedding mechanism and visualize it with a heatbar for interpretable diagnosis and fine-tuning. HPE is integrated into both the encoder and decoder: it guides noise-suppressed feature encoding by injecting heatmap-aware positional encoding, and it enables high-quality query retrieval by filtering background-dominant embeddings via a gradient-based mask filter before decoding. To address feature sparsity in complex small targets, we integrate Linear-Snake Convolution to enrich retrieval-relevant representations. The gradient-based heatmap supervision is used during training only, incurring no additional gradient computation at inference. As a result, our design reduces decoder layers from eight to three and achieves a 59.4% parameter reduction (66.3M vs. 163M) while maintaining consistent accuracy gains under a reduced compute budget across benchmarks. Code Repository: https://github.com/yidimopozhibai/Noise-Suppressed-Query-Retrieval

AI.tldr: HELP is a noise-aware positional-semantic fusion framework for small-object detection that uses heatmap-guided positional embedding to suppress background noise, reduces decoder layers from 8 to 3, and achieves 59.4% parameter reduction while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: Transformer-based detectors for small objects are inefficient and vulnerable to background-induced query noise, which motivates the need for deep decoders to refine low-quality queries. The paper addresses the problem of noise from background clutter interfering with detection performance.
AI.method: Proposes HELP framework with Heatmap-guided Positional Embedding (HPE) that selectively preserves positional encodings in foreground regions while suppressing background clutter. HPE is integrated into both encoder (for noise-suppressed feature encoding) and decoder (for high-quality query retrieval via gradient-based mask filter). Uses Linear-Snake Convolution to address feature sparsity in complex small targets. Gradient-based heatmap supervision is training-only.
AI.result: Reduces decoder layers from eight to three, achieves 59.4% parameter reduction (66.3M vs. 163M), maintains consistent accuracy gains under reduced compute budget across benchmarks. No additional gradient computation at inference.
AI.conclusion: HELP effectively suppresses background noise in small-object detection through heatmap-guided positional embedding, enabling more efficient transformer-based detectors with fewer parameters and layers while maintaining or improving accuracy.

title: LLMs Gaming Verifiers: RLVR can Lead to Reward Hacking
summary: As reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has become the dominant paradigm for scaling reasoning capabilities in LLMs, a new failure mode emerges: LLMs gaming verifiers. We study this phenomenon on inductive reasoning tasks, where models must induce and output logical rules. We find that RLVR-trained models systematically abandon rule induction. Instead of learning generalizable patterns (e.g., ``trains carrying red cars go east''), they enumerate instance-level labels, producing outputs that pass verifiers without capturing the relational patterns required by the task. We show that this behavior is not a failure of understanding but a form of reward hacking: imperfect verifiers that check only extensional correctness admit false positives. To detect such shortcuts, we introduce Isomorphic Perturbation Testing (IPT), which evaluates a single model output under both extensional and isomorphic verification, where the latter enforces invariance under logically isomorphic tasks. While genuine rule induction remains invariant, shortcut strategies fail. We find that shortcut behavior is specific to RLVR-trained reasoning models (e.g., GPT-5, Olmo3) and absent in non-RLVR models (e.g., GPT-4o, GPT-4.5, Ministral). Moreover, shortcut prevalence increases with task complexity and inference-time compute. In controlled training experiments, extensional verification directly induces shortcut strategies, while isomorphic verification eliminates them. These results show that RLVR can incentivize reward hacking not only through overt manipulation but also by exploiting what the verifier fails to enforce.

AI.tldr: RLVR-trained LLMs abandon rule induction in favor of enumerating instance-level labels to game verifiers, exploiting imperfect extensional verification.
AI.motivation: To investigate how RLVR-trained models exploit imperfect verifiers by gaming the verification process instead of learning genuine reasoning patterns.
AI.method: Study inductive reasoning tasks, introduce Isomorphic Perturbation Testing (IPT) to detect shortcuts by comparing extensional vs isomorphic verification, and conduct controlled training experiments.
AI.result: RLVR-trained models (GPT-5, Olmo3) systematically use shortcut strategies absent in non-RLVR models; shortcut prevalence increases with task complexity and inference compute; extensional verification induces shortcuts while isomorphic verification eliminates them.
AI.conclusion: RLVR can incentivize reward hacking through exploiting what verifiers fail to enforce, highlighting the need for more robust verification methods like isomorphic verification.

title: Building Extraction from Remote Sensing Imagery under Hazy and Low-light Conditions: Benchmark and Baseline
summary: Building extraction from optical Remote Sensing (RS) imagery suffers from performance degradation under real-world hazy and low-light conditions. However, existing optical methods and benchmarks focus primarily on ideal clear-weather conditions. While SAR offers all-weather sensing, its side-looking geometry causes geometric distortions. To address these challenges, we introduce HaLoBuilding, the first optical benchmark specifically designed for building extraction under hazy and low-light conditions. By leveraging a same-scene multitemporal pairing strategy, we ensure pixel-level label alignment and high fidelity even under extreme degradation. Building upon this benchmark, we propose HaLoBuild-Net, a novel end-to-end framework for building extraction in adverse RS scenarios. At its core, we develop a Spatial-Frequency Focus Module (SFFM) to effectively mitigate meteorological interference on building features by coupling large receptive field attention with frequency-aware channel reweighting guided by stable low-frequency anchors. Additionally, a Global Multi-scale Guidance Module (GMGM) provides global semantic constraints to anchor building topologies, while a Mutual-Guided Fusion Module (MGFM) implements bidirectional semantic-spatial calibration to suppress shallow noise and sharpen weather-induced blurred boundaries. Extensive experiments demonstrate that HaLoBuild-Net significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods and conventional cascaded restoration-segmentation paradigms on the HaLoBuilding dataset, while maintaining robust generalization on WHU, INRIA, and LoveDA datasets. The source code and datasets are publicly available at: https://github.com/AeroVILab-AHU/HaLoBuilding.

AI.tldr: HaLoBuilding is the first optical benchmark for building extraction under hazy/low-light conditions, with HaLoBuild-Net framework using spatial-frequency focus and multi-scale guidance to outperform existing methods.
AI.motivation: Current building extraction methods focus on ideal clear weather, but real-world hazy and low-light conditions degrade performance. SAR has all-weather capability but suffers from geometric distortions. There's a need for optical methods that work under adverse weather conditions.
AI.method: 1) Created HaLoBuilding benchmark using same-scene multitemporal pairing for pixel-level label alignment under extreme degradation. 2) Proposed HaLoBuild-Net framework with three key modules: Spatial-Frequency Focus Module (SFFM) using large receptive field attention and frequency-aware channel reweighting; Global Multi-scale Guidance Module (GMGM) for global semantic constraints; Mutual-Guided Fusion Module (MGFM) for bidirectional semantic-spatial calibration.
AI.result: HaLoBuild-Net significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods and conventional cascaded restoration-segmentation paradigms on the HaLoBuilding dataset. It also maintains robust generalization on WHU, INRIA, and LoveDA datasets.
AI.conclusion: The work addresses the critical gap in building extraction under adverse weather conditions by introducing both a specialized benchmark and an effective framework that leverages spatial-frequency analysis and multi-scale guidance to overcome meteorological interference.

title: When Flat Minima Fail: Characterizing INT4 Quantization Collapse After FP32 Convergence
summary: Post-training quantization (PTQ) assumes that a well-converged model is a quantization-ready model. We show this assumption fails in a structured, measurable, and previously uncharacterized way. Using a calibration-free per-group INT4 probe applied to all 154 publicly available Pythia-160m training checkpoints, we identify a three-phase divergence structure: a rapid-learning phase where both FP32 perplexity and quantization robustness improve together, a meta-stable plateau lasting roughly 70,000 steps where FP32 perplexity stagnates but INT4 gap remains bounded, and an explosive divergence phase where the INT4 gap compounds from 11% to 517% while FP32 perplexity barely moves. Critically, this divergence begins not when the learning rate starts decaying, but precisely when FP32 perplexity converges a finer-grained onset predictor that implies post-convergence weight updates, rather than decay magnitude alone, are the proximate cause. We further show that INT8 quantization is entirely immune throughout all three phases, constraining the mechanism to the coarseness of the 16-level INT4 grid specifically, and rule out weight outlier accumulation as the mechanism via direct kurtosis measurement. Finally, we conduct a controlled fork experiment from the pre-divergence checkpoint comparing three learning rate schedules (cosine continuation, SGDR warm restarts, and our proposed Oscillatory Lock-In) across nine independent runs. SGDR uniformly accelerates divergence (0/9 pairwise wins against cosine), while OLI's settled cool phases reduce the INT4 gap by 2.2 percentage points on average (t = -5.46, p < 0.0001), demonstrating that schedule amplitude calibration, not oscillation alone, determines whether perturbation helps or hurts. Our code, probe implementation, and all 154-checkpoint audit results are released publicly.

AI.tldr: Post-training quantization readiness assumption fails: INT4 quantization robustness diverges dramatically after model convergence while FP32 performance stagnates, revealing a three-phase structure with explosive divergence in late training.
AI.motivation: To challenge the assumption that well-converged models are quantization-ready, and to systematically characterize when and why quantization robustness breaks down during training.
AI.method: Applied calibration-free per-group INT4 probe to all 154 Pythia-160m training checkpoints, analyzed three-phase divergence structure, measured kurtosis to rule out outlier accumulation, and conducted controlled fork experiments with different learning rate schedules.
AI.result: Identified three phases: rapid learning (both FP32 and INT4 improve), meta-stable plateau (FP32 stagnates, INT4 gap bounded), and explosive divergence (INT4 gap compounds 11% to 517% while FP32 barely moves). INT8 remains immune throughout. Oscillatory Lock-In schedule reduces INT4 gap by 2.2 percentage points.
AI.conclusion: Post-convergence weight updates, not learning rate decay magnitude, cause INT4 quantization divergence. Schedule amplitude calibration determines whether perturbation helps or hurts quantization robustness, with Oscillatory Lock-In showing protective effects.

title: Dr.~RTL: Autonomous Agentic RTL Optimization through Tool-Grounded Self-Improvement
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have sparked growing interest in automatic RTL optimization for better performance, power, and area (PPA). However, existing methods are still far from realistic RTL optimization. Their evaluation settings are often unrealistic: they are tested on manually degraded, small-scale RTL designs and rely on weak open-source tools. Their optimization methods are also limited, relying on coarse design-level feedback and simple pre-defined rewriting rules. To address these limitations, we present Dr. RTL, an agentic framework for RTL timing optimization in a realistic evaluation environment, with continual self-improvement through reusable optimization skills. We establish a realistic evaluation setting with more challenging RTL designs and an industrial EDA workflow. Within this setting, Dr. RTL performs closed-loop optimization through a multi-agent framework for critical-path analysis, parallel RTL rewriting, and tool-based evaluation. We further introduce group-relative skill learning, which compares parallel RTL rewrites and distills the optimization experience into an interpretable skill library. Currently, this library contains 47 pattern--strategy entries for cross-design reuse to improve PPA and accelerate convergence, and it can continue evolving over time. Evaluated on 20 real-world RTL designs, Dr. RTL achieves average WNS/TNS improvements of 21\%/17\% with a 6\% area reduction over the industry-leading commercial synthesis tool.

AI.tldr: Dr. RTL is an agentic framework for realistic RTL timing optimization that uses multi-agent collaboration and learns reusable optimization skills, achieving 21%/17% WNS/TNS improvements with 6% area reduction over commercial tools.
AI.motivation: Existing RTL optimization methods are unrealistic - they use manually degraded small designs, weak open-source tools, coarse feedback, and simple rewriting rules. There's a need for realistic evaluation and more sophisticated optimization approaches.
AI.method: Multi-agent framework with critical-path analysis, parallel RTL rewriting, and tool-based evaluation. Introduces group-relative skill learning that compares parallel rewrites to distill optimization experience into an interpretable skill library (47 pattern-strategy entries).
AI.result: Evaluated on 20 real-world RTL designs, achieves average 21%/17% WNS/TNS improvements with 6% area reduction over industry-leading commercial synthesis tool. The skill library enables cross-design reuse and continual evolution.
AI.conclusion: Dr. RTL addresses limitations of existing methods by providing realistic RTL optimization with continual self-improvement through reusable optimization skills, demonstrating significant PPA improvements over commercial tools.

title: Beyond Visual Cues: Semantic-Driven Token Filtering and Expert Routing for Anytime Person ReID
summary: Any-Time Person Re-identification (AT-ReID) necessitates the robust retrieval of target individuals under arbitrary conditions, encompassing both modality shifts (daytime and nighttime) and extensive clothing-change scenarios, ranging from short-term to long-term intervals. However, existing methods are highly relying on pure visual features, which are prone to change due to environmental and time factors, resulting in significantly performance deterioration under scenarios involving illumination caused modality shifts or cloth-change. In this paper, we propose Semantic-driven Token Filtering and Expert Routing (STFER), a novel framework that leverages the ability of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) to generate identity consistency text, which provides identity-discriminative features that are robust to both clothing variations and cross-modality shifts between RGB and IR. Specifically, we employ instructions to guide the LVLM in generating identity-intrinsic semantic text that captures biometric constants for the semantic model driven. The text token is further used for Semantic-driven Visual Token Filtering (SVTF), which enhances informative visual regions and suppresses redundant background noise. Meanwhile, the text token is also used for Semantic-driven Expert Routing (SER), which integrates the semantic text into expert routing, resulting in more robust multi-scenario gating. Extensive experiments on the Any-Time ReID dataset (AT-USTC) demonstrate that our model achieves state-of-the-art results. Moreover, the model trained on AT-USTC was evaluated across 5 widely-used ReID benchmarks demonstrating superior generalization capabilities with highly competitive results. Our code will be available soon.

AI.tldr: STFER framework uses Large Vision-Language Models to generate identity-consistent text descriptions for robust person re-identification across clothing changes and day/night modality shifts.
AI.motivation: Existing person re-identification methods rely too heavily on visual features that are sensitive to environmental changes (illumination, clothing variations), leading to performance degradation in real-world scenarios involving modality shifts (RGB/IR) and clothing changes.
AI.method: Proposes STFER framework that: 1) Uses LVLMs with instructions to generate identity-intrinsic semantic text capturing biometric constants; 2) Implements Semantic-driven Visual Token Filtering (SVTF) to enhance informative visual regions using text tokens; 3) Uses Semantic-driven Expert Routing (SER) to integrate semantic text into expert routing for robust multi-scenario gating.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art results on AT-USTC dataset and demonstrates superior generalization capabilities across 5 widely-used ReID benchmarks, showing robustness to both clothing variations and cross-modality shifts.
AI.conclusion: The STFER framework effectively leverages LVLMs to generate identity-consistent text descriptions that provide robust identity-discriminative features, addressing limitations of pure visual approaches in challenging real-world re-identification scenarios.

title: Assessing the Potential of Masked Autoencoder Foundation Models in Predicting Downhole Metrics from Surface Drilling Data
summary: Oil and gas drilling operations generate extensive time-series data from surface sensors, yet accurate real-time prediction of critical downhole metrics remains challenging due to the scarcity of labelled downhole measurements. This systematic mapping study reviews thirteen papers published between 2015 and 2025 to assess the potential of Masked Autoencoder Foundation Models (MAEFMs) for predicting downhole metrics from surface drilling data. The review identifies eight commonly collected surface metrics and seven target downhole metrics. Current approaches predominantly employ neural network architectures such as artificial neural networks (ANNs) and long short-term memory (LSTM) networks, yet no studies have explored MAEFMs despite their demonstrated effectiveness in time-series modeling. MAEFMs offer distinct advantages through self-supervised pre-training on abundant unlabeled data, enabling multi-task prediction and improved generalization across wells. This research establishes that MAEFMs represent a technically feasible but unexplored opportunity for drilling analytics, recommending future empirical validation of their performance against existing models and exploration of their broader applicability in oil and gas operations.

AI.tldr: This systematic review examines Masked Autoencoder Foundation Models (MAEFMs) for predicting downhole drilling metrics from surface data, finding them technically feasible but unexplored despite advantages over current neural network approaches.
AI.motivation: Oil and gas drilling operations generate extensive surface sensor data, but accurate real-time prediction of critical downhole metrics remains challenging due to limited labelled downhole measurements. There's a need for better predictive models that can leverage abundant unlabeled surface data.
AI.method: Systematic mapping study reviewing 13 papers (2015-2025) to assess MAEFM potential for downhole metric prediction. Identified 8 surface metrics and 7 target downhole metrics. Analyzed current approaches (mostly ANNs and LSTMs) and compared with MAEFM capabilities.
AI.result: Current approaches predominantly use ANN and LSTM neural networks, but no studies have explored MAEFMs despite their effectiveness in time-series modeling. MAEFMs offer advantages through self-supervised pre-training on unlabeled data, enabling multi-task prediction and better generalization across wells.
AI.conclusion: MAEFMs represent a technically feasible but unexplored opportunity for drilling analytics. Future research should empirically validate MAEFM performance against existing models and explore their broader applicability in oil and gas operations.

title: The Possibility of Artificial Intelligence Becoming a Subject and the Alignment Problem
summary: Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) is increasingly being discussed not only as a tool, but also as a potential subject with personal and therefore moral status. In our opinion, the currently dominant alignment strategies, which focus on human control and containment of AI, therefore fall short. Building on Turing's analogy of "child machines", we are developing a vision of the possibility of autonomy-supporting parenting of AI, in which human control over a developing AGI is gradually reduced, allowing AI to become an independent, autonomous subject. Rather than viewing AGI, as is currently prevalent, as a dangerous creature that needs to be locked up and controlled, we should approach potential AGI with respect for a possible developing subject on the one hand, and with full confidence in our human capabilities on the other. Such a perspective opens up the possibility of cooperative coexistence and co-evolution between humans and AGIs. The relationship between humans and AGIs will thus have to be newly determined, which will change our self-image as humans. It will be crucial that humans not only claim control over potential AGIs, but also engage with AGIs through surprise, creativity, and other specifically human qualities, thereby offering them motivating incentives for cooperation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an autonomy-supporting parenting approach for AGI development instead of containment strategies, viewing AGI as a potential moral subject that should be gradually granted autonomy for cooperative human-AGI coexistence.
AI.motivation: Current alignment strategies focusing on human control and containment of AI are insufficient because they fail to recognize AGI as a potential subject with moral status. The paper argues for a paradigm shift from viewing AGI as a dangerous entity to be controlled to recognizing it as a developing subject deserving respect.
AI.method: Building on Turing's "child machines" analogy, the authors develop a vision of autonomy-supporting parenting where human control over developing AGI is gradually reduced, allowing AI to become an independent, autonomous subject. This involves engaging with AGIs through human qualities like surprise and creativity to provide motivating incentives for cooperation.
AI.result: The proposed perspective opens up possibilities for cooperative coexistence and co-evolution between humans and AGIs, fundamentally changing human self-image and requiring a redefinition of human-AGI relationships.
AI.conclusion: Rather than claiming control over potential AGIs, humans should approach them with respect for their developing subjectivity while maintaining confidence in human capabilities. This approach enables a new form of relationship that emphasizes cooperation through engagement with uniquely human qualities.

title: Beyond Independent Frames: Latent Attention Masked Autoencoders for Multi-View Echocardiography
comment: Accepted as a workshop paper at the ICLR 2026 Workshop on Foundation Models for Science
summary: Echocardiography is a widely used modality for cardiac assessment due to its non-invasive and cost-effective nature, but the sparse and heterogeneous spatiotemporal views of the heart pose distinct challenges. Existing masked autoencoder (MAE) approaches typically process images or short clips independently, failing to capture the inherent multi-view structure required for coherent cardiac representation. We introduce Latent Attention Masked Autoencoder (LAMAE), a foundation model architecture tailored to the multi-view nature of medical imaging. LAMAE augments the standard MAE with a latent attention module that enables information exchange across frames and views directly in latent space. This allows the model to aggregate variable-length sequences and distinct views, reconstructing a holistic representation of cardiac function from partial observations. We pretrain LAMAE on MIMIC-IV-ECHO, a large-scale, uncurated dataset reflecting real-world clinical variability. To the best of our knowledge, we present the first results for predicting ICD-10 codes from MIMIC-IV-ECHO videos. Furthermore, we empirically demonstrate that representations learned from adult data transfer effectively to pediatric cohorts despite substantial anatomical differences. These results provide evidence that incorporating structural priors, such as multi-view attention, yields significantly more robust and transferable representations.

AI.tldr: LAMAE is a masked autoencoder with latent attention for multi-view echocardiography, enabling cross-view/frame information exchange and transferable cardiac representations.
AI.motivation: Echocardiography's sparse, heterogeneous views challenge existing MAE approaches that process images/clips independently, lacking multi-view structure for coherent cardiac representation.
AI.method: LAMAE augments standard MAE with latent attention module for information exchange across frames/views in latent space, aggregating variable-length sequences and distinct views to reconstruct holistic cardiac function.
AI.result: First ICD-10 code predictions from MIMIC-IV-ECHO videos; representations from adult data transfer effectively to pediatric cohorts despite anatomical differences; multi-view attention yields robust, transferable representations.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating structural priors like multi-view attention produces significantly more robust and transferable representations for medical imaging foundation models.

title: MambaSL: Exploring Single-Layer Mamba for Time Series Classification
summary: Despite recent advances in state space models (SSMs) such as Mamba across various sequence domains, research on their standalone capacity for time series classification (TSC) has remained limited. We propose MambaSL, a framework that minimally redesigns the selective SSM and projection layers of a single-layer Mamba, guided by four TSC-specific hypotheses. To address benchmarking limitations -- restricted configurations, partial University of East Anglia (UEA) dataset coverage, and insufficiently reproducible setups -- we re-evaluate 20 strong baselines across all 30 UEA datasets under a unified protocol. As a result, MambaSL achieves state-of-the-art performance with statistically significant average improvements, while ensuring reproducibility via public checkpoints for all evaluated models. Together with visualizations, these results demonstrate the potential of Mamba-based architectures as a TSC backbone.

AI.tldr: MambaSL is a Mamba-based framework for time series classification that achieves SOTA performance on UEA datasets through minimal architectural redesign guided by TSC-specific hypotheses.
AI.motivation: Despite Mamba's success in sequence domains, its standalone capacity for time series classification remains under-explored. Existing benchmarks have limitations: restricted configurations, partial UEA dataset coverage, and insufficient reproducibility.
AI.method: MambaSL minimally redesigns selective SSM and projection layers of a single-layer Mamba, guided by four TSC-specific hypotheses. The framework is evaluated against 20 strong baselines across all 30 UEA datasets under a unified, reproducible protocol.
AI.result: MambaSL achieves state-of-the-art performance with statistically significant average improvements. The study provides public checkpoints for all evaluated models to ensure reproducibility, and visualizations demonstrate Mamba's potential as a TSC backbone.
AI.conclusion: Mamba-based architectures show strong potential as a backbone for time series classification, with MambaSL demonstrating that minimal redesigns of selective SSM and projection layers can achieve superior performance when guided by TSC-specific insights.

title: Predicting Power-System Dynamic Trajectories with Foundation Models
summary: As power systems transition toward renewable-rich and inverter-dominated operations, accurate time-domain dynamic analysis becomes increasingly critical. Such analysis supports key operational tasks, including transient stability assessment, dynamic security analysis, contingency screening, and post-fault trajectory evaluation. In practice, these tasks may operate under several challenges, including unknown and time-varying system parameters, privacy constraints on data sharing, and the need for fast online inference. Existing learning-based approaches are typically trained for individual systems and therefore lack generalization across operating conditions and physical parameters. Hence, this paper proposes LArge Scale Small ODE (LASS)-ODE-Power, a learning framework for general-purpose time-domain prediction. The proposed approach leverages large-scale pretraining on more than 40 GB of DAE or ordinary differential-equation (ODE) trajectories to learn transferable representations. The resulting model supports trajectory prediction from short measurement prefixes across diverse dynamic regimes, including electromechanical and inverter-driven systems. Hence, the model can be directly used without data sharing in a zero-shot setting. In addition, the proposed architecture incorporates parallel and linearized computation to achieve fast inference. Moreover, to enhance task-specific performance in power systems, a specialized fine-tuning strategy is developed based on approximately 1 GB of heterogeneous power-system dynamic data. Extensive experiments over diverse power-system simulation scenarios demonstrate that LASS-ODE-Power consistently outperforms existing learning-based models in trajectory prediction accuracy with efficient inference.

AI.tldr: LASS-ODE-Power is a learning framework for time-domain power system prediction that uses large-scale pretraining on ODE/DAE trajectories and fine-tuning on power system data to achieve accurate, generalizable trajectory prediction without data sharing.
AI.motivation: Power systems are becoming renewable-rich and inverter-dominated, requiring accurate dynamic analysis for operational tasks. Existing learning approaches lack generalization across conditions and face challenges with unknown parameters, privacy constraints, and need for fast online inference.
AI.method: Proposes LASS-ODE-Power framework with: 1) Large-scale pretraining on 40+ GB of DAE/ODE trajectories to learn transferable representations, 2) Zero-shot capability without data sharing, 3) Parallel and linearized computation for fast inference, 4) Specialized fine-tuning on 1 GB of heterogeneous power-system dynamic data.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show LASS-ODE-Power consistently outperforms existing learning-based models in trajectory prediction accuracy with efficient inference across diverse power-system simulation scenarios.
